text: ### Translation of 异闻总录卷之一 During the Da Guan era, in the capital city, a doctor named Geng Yu bought a beautiful and cunning servant girl, and she had been with him for a year. She once stood at the door, and a child passed by, recognizing her as his mother, fondly attached and reluctant to leave, and the servant girl also comforted and pitied him. The child returned home and told his father, "My mother is in a certain house." At the time, his mother had died, and the mourning period had ended, but the father did not believe him. He went to the burial place to check, and it seemed to have been disturbed by thieves, and the body was not there. He then returned home with the child and visited the neighbors of the Geng family, secretly inquiring about the servant girl's name and surname, and found out that she was truly his wife. He then pretended to be a peddler, loitering on the road, waiting for her to come out and see her. His wife called him forward, and they recounted their separation, followed by tears. She told others, "This is my husband, and the smaller one is my son!" Geng Yu heard this and became angry, scolding him, "When I bought you last year, you did not have a husband, and there are contracts and brokers who can verify this, so how dare you!" The husband took the case to the Kaifeng Prefecture, tracing the origin, but the servant girl was confused and could not recall, only saying that she had walked to a bridge, got lost, and was led away by a matchmaker, forced by hunger and starvation, so she sold herself. The matchmaker also said that she really met her at Guangbei Bridge, where she sought to return home for food, and thus sold herself to pay off debts. The magistrate did not have time to investigate the details, ordering the husband to pay the remaining price to Geng Yu and take his wife, but Geng Yu was not satisfied. The husband then took the case to the Imperial Censorate, but the proceedings were not completed, and the woman was lost again, so the lawsuit ended. Within a year, Geng Yu died, and his family also declined.

During the Xuanhe period, Dong, a scholar from the village, was studying at the prefectural school. One day, while he went to the restroom, he saw a white-clad woman lingering in front of him. He asked her why she was there, and she replied, "I am a woman from the vegetable garden. My husband has not yet died, but I am pitifully without a place to return to." Dong spoke with her and told her about the place where they studied. That night, she came to his room and slept with him. Soon after, Dong fell ill, and his fellow students, knowing what had happened, reported it to the teacher. The teacher came to Dong's room and scolded him, saying, "How could a scholar be possessed by other beings?" The teacher asked Dong what he had, and Dong said he had left a loose-fitting garment behind. The teacher took it and saw that it was dirty and without seams. He ordered it to be thrown into the fire and sent the students to trace the trail. An old gardener said, "Previously, a little boy was herding sheep, and a female sheep fell into the well at the western gate. It could not be retrieved. Now, the white-clad woman has appeared, could she be a ghost?" The teacher called a Taoist priest to recite spells and threw black beans into the well. The ghost no longer came, but Dong also died.

In the sixth year of the Shaoxing era, a family of farmers in Yudan, a village in Jiangxi, had already gone to sleep. A herdsman, tending to the cattle in the barn, heard someone knocking on the door and quickly got up to see. He saw hundreds of strong men, all wearing colorful armor and red helmets, suddenly burst into the barn. They then disappeared. By morning, fifty cows in the pen were all dead, it was said to be due to epidemic spirits. The Zhi Mountain is located one li north of the city. All around it are burial grounds, with Buddhist temples and side rooms filled with crowded coffins. In the wind and snow, one could always hear chirping sounds, indicating that it was a ghostly area. In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era, the Deputy Magistrate Ren Liangchen Berian lost his son. He entered the temple to arrange a water-land ritual. Not yet halfway through the night, the entire temple heard laughing and joking at the foot of the mountain, with people coming and going and asking after each other's welfare. A servant of Cheng Cibu, who was guarding a tomb, returned from a side path near a fork in the road. He encountered two people walking together, who sternly said, "We were summoned to gather in the temple, and now we are going there. What are you doing here, and how dare you come here?" They chased him and wanted to hit him, and he fled. Just then, a campfire came out from the temple, and he managed to escape.

Fuzhou Shubi, twenty miles away from the city, is a village surrounded by lush forests, where the prominent Rao family resides. Once, family members went for a walk in the forest and saw a willow tree that was hollow inside and could hold water, serving as a mirror. The daughter-in-law playfully peered into it and immediately fell ill. She returned home and lay in a daze, no longer aware of anything. Then, there was a voice speaking from the air, interacting with people. It could hear human singing and respond in harmony, turning gracefully like a rising melody, with lingering resonance. If there was a slight error in the melody, it would laugh and point out the mistake. It could discuss literature and poetry, generally making sense, yet it was so close yet invisible in form. If servants or concubines did something wrong, it would speak openly to the master, even if something was done hundreds of miles away, it would immediately reveal it, nothing was hidden or concealed. The family accumulated anger and frustration, resorting to violence, prayer, and various other methods, but ultimately achieving nothing. After several years, the Rao family burned incense and prayed, saying, "We have been favored by your divine presence for a long time. Humans and spirits have different paths, and we fear to be disrespectful, which would shame the spirit. We wish to build a new temple in the mountains, with incense, altars, and offerings, and hope that the spirit will move to the new temple, so that both parties can be content. Is this not a good plan?" The spirit agreed, and from then on, there was no further contact. The Rao family was pleased with their plan and built a very grand temple, choosing a day to invite the spirit to the temple. Five days later, it arrived, and its playful remarks were as before. The elder of the Rao family scolded it, saying, "You are now being worshipped in the temple, why do you still torment us?" It laughed and said, "Am I a fool? With such a grand hall and large house, why would I leave and go to a small temple?" The Rao family became increasingly disheartened. Until the daughter-in-law died, the spirit finally left, and the family declined as a result.

A minor official from Wuchang, Li, dreamed of being summoned to the underworld. The master asked him, "When you were the son of the Zhang family in your previous life, how could you push your wife into the water?" Li suddenly remembered the incident in his dream and replied, "My wife fell into the water and died accidentally; I did not push her." The master sought evidence from the local mountain and river deities, which matched what Li said, and he was then released. Some days later, he met a woman at an inn who claimed to be his wife from a previous life. She clung to him, unwilling to let go, and they were intimate as if they were alive. All relatives and friends knew about it, and after several years, she finally left. Li did not marry again for the rest of his life. Although he had no other suffering, he often suffered from lower back pain. He made two wooden clubs, hollowed out the inside, and struck himself dozens of times each day, which alleviated the pain slightly. This was likely caused by the lingering influence of the ghostly atmosphere.

The Principal of the Prefectural School in Hongzhou, Zhang, was inherently stingy and became even more so as he grew older. Even when students requested leave, he was reluctant to grant it. When the school official granted five days, he reduced it to three; when granted three days, he reduced it to two. All the rest were the same, and the people resented him. There was a man named Ghost Zhang, who was called so because his appearance resembled a ghost. The people made him pretend to be a demon from the underworld pursuing a ghost to frighten Old Zhang. Ghost Zhang readily agreed, saying, "I will obey your command, but to make the false seem real, I need a document from the underworld." The people asked, "What should the format of the document be?" He replied, "I have seen it done before." He then took paper, wrote finely with white alum, and signed it at the end. That night, he went to the prefectural school, where the school gate was already locked. Ghost Zhang entered through a crack, and the people were startled. Old Zhang saw him and angrily said, "You beast, how dare you! Surely the others made you come at night to frighten me." Ghost Zhang smiled and said, "I am here with a document from the King of Hell to pursue you." Old Zhang asked for the document but had not finished reading it when Ghost Zhang revealed his head covering, showing two horns across his head. Old Zhang screamed in shock and died immediately. Ghost Zhang emerged and stood in the courtyard, saying, "I am truly a bull-headed jailer. Yesterday, I was ordered to pursue this old man, but I accidentally lost the talisman while crossing water and have been afraid to return for twenty years. Thanks to the efforts of the scholars, I was able to return with the message. Now, the false seems real!" He bowed and thanked them before disappearing. Chen Zhengmin's *Tun Zhai Xian Lian Ji* records that Li Anshi, while studying at the Imperial Academy, was played a trick by his fellow students using a ghostly talisman that led to his death, which is somewhat similar to this story, but they are separate incidents.

In the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era, Li Deyu was appointed to an official position in Lin'an and stayed at the White Wall Camp, where he shared accommodations with Yao, the magistrate of Fuzhou. It was the height of summer, and every evening they would cool off in the back pavilion. Yao's old friend, Jia, the county magistrate, came to handle matters related to the lost official documents. Their residences were only a hundred steps apart, and Jia would go out in the morning and return in the evening, necessarily visiting Yao for a night chat, through which Li Deyu came to know him. Jia, the county magistrate, was from Chang'an, and he talked about the prosperity of the ancient capital and its well-known places, such as the Lishan Palace, in a way that was endlessly fascinating. He had also served as the magistrate of Jianyun and told stories about the ghostly immortal Huaying, which were particularly well-substantiated. Li Deyu and Yao bought more wine and set out fruit to socialize with him. They continued to meet like this for two months before eventually parting ways. Two years later, Li Deyu worked in the office responsible for editing imperial edicts. One day, he casually discussed strange and supernatural matters with his colleague Tang Xindao. Tang detailed the story of Huaying, and Li Deyu responded as if echoing. Tang asked, "How do you know about this?" Li Deyu told him what he had heard. Tang was startled and said, "Isn't he the tall, bearded man?" Li Deyu replied, "Yes." "Is he from Shaanxi?" "Yes," Li Deyu confirmed. Tang continued, "This man resigned from his position in Jianyun and died shortly after. His brother buried him in a desolate place, and I attended his funeral before returning. It has been ten years now. How can there be someone like you describe?!" Li Deyu felt a sudden fear, his hair standing on end. After careful thought, he reflected, "We have been together for so long, and he never came during the day. Even though we spoke and laughed together in the same room, he always sat far from the light, and I never really saw his face clearly. Now I realize he was a ghost." Yao passed away several years after they parted. (Jia's name was Xiu, and his courtesy name was Yu Zhong.)

At the Baogqi Pavilion by the Yangtze River in Fengcheng County, during the third year of the Jianyan era (1129 AD), the residents heard the call of Zhu Sheshi for several consecutive nights but did not see anyone. Shortly after, the new governor of Ganzhou, Feng Jizhou, who had been appointed as a compiler or historian, moored his boat at the pavilion. A servant named Zhu, who accompanied him, drowned on August 4th. The magistrate of Jiaxing, Tao Tan, had a son who fell ill with a very unusual disease. His appearance, speech, and laughter were not like his usual self. Concerned, Tao Tan consulted sorcerers and shamans for exorcism, trying every method, but all attempts failed to cure his son. Coincidentally, Dharma Master Yuanjing from Tianzhu (India) arrived in the area. Yuanjing was skilled in water spells, and those who drank the water he prepared would immediately recover. The people of Wu revered and respected him. Tao Tan, who had long heard of his name, went to pay respects and presented a detailed account, saying: "My son first fell ill when a woman from outside came and teased him. After some time, they both went out, and as they walked to the riverside, she left behind a poem that said: 'Born as a person of the Wood element, I die as a lonely ghost. The gate of the underworld opens for the long night, the bed and curtains await your arrival.' Since then, she has come repeatedly and said: 'In the mid-winter month, on the fifteenth day, when the moon is full, carriages and horses will come to welcome you.' The time of the evil spirit is approaching, and I do not know what to do. I beg for your compassion." Yuanjing agreed and went with a staff to Tao Tan's home. He cleared the ground to set up an altar, placed an image of Guanyin Bodhisattva, took the water from the willow branch, and sprinkled it while reciting spells. He circled the altar three times and left. That night, the child slept peacefully.

The next day, he sat in the lotus position and led the child, asking, "Where do you reside, and how did you come here?" The child replied, "In the east of Kuaiji, on the sunny side of Bian Mountain, that is my home, with ancient trees standing tall." He then asked the child's name and was told, "In the place on Wu Wang Mountain where no one is, I have danced many times by the wind." He said, "Are you of the surname Liu?" The child smiled brightly. He said, "Since the beginning of time, you have been lost and followed after things, being turned and drowned in lust and evil. You have wandered through countless eons, not freeing yourself, entering the realm of demons, causing harm, and extending to the innocent. You must now know that demons are the realm of dharma. I will proclaim the Secret Mantra of the Lengyan Sutra for you. Listen carefully, deeply repent, cleanse your past sins, and return to your original pure and clear consciousness." The child then wept and said no more. That night, he told the child, "The merit of your father's eloquence and devotion is unmatched. I will leave now, but I will return in two days. After spending so much time with you, I cannot part easily. I wish to raise a cup for a farewell." They then drank together, and after finishing, he composed a poem: "The middle of winter, the seventh day of the seventh month is a good time. We have no chance to meet again on the river. A cup of wine at the parting place, to part ways with you over a thousand miles." He then left and was never seen again. (Recorded by Qin Guan)

During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, Jiang Gui, acting as an official under the Zhe Dong Anfu Si, resided in the former office of the Department of Ceremonies in Kuaiji. His three sons, all around ten years old, woke up early and went to a small pavilion in the middle hall. There, they saw a woman dressed in a silk robe and a light-colored skirt, who was dressing and combing her hair. Surprised that she was not a member of their family, the sons went into the room to tell their father. Jiang Gui quickly got up to look and saw the woman's back, who then disappeared beside the bed of an old woman. He called the old woman to speak with her, and she said, "It is still dark, and the woman picked a branch of peach blossoms outside the window, pinned it to her hair, smiled, and entered. She fell asleep again momentarily and I do not know who she was." Jiang Gui asked the old gatekeeper, who replied, "Twenty years ago, Liu Yicao lived here, and his wife died due to childbirth difficulties in this room. The woman you saw is likely her." People say that ghosts fear peach blossoms, but this belief is illogical. (As told by Jiang Mingyu)

In the tenth year of the Shaoxing era, Zhang Yuandao was appointed as the supervisor of tea and horse affairs in the Qin region. He traveled from Huaiyin to take up his post, accompanied by a veteran soldier named Zhang Chao, who was a stable hand and carried his wife and children with him on the journey. His young daughter, who was only seven years old, turned restlessly in bed at night and could not sleep. Her mother patted her and said, "It's past midnight; it's time to sleep." The girl smiled and said, "The water outside is very clear, with countless small fish swimming in it. I am enjoying the view and play, and I want to think of a way to catch them. I don't have time to go to bed yet!" Thinking her words were delirious, her mother hugged her tightly. After a long time, she became tired. When she woke up, she had disappeared. They searched all night. At dawn, they looked behind the house and saw an old well about several steps away. Testing it with a pole, it seemed like a person. Going out to look, it was indeed the girl, and she could not be saved. It turned out that when she was seen deliriously last night, her soul had already been taken by the spirits.

The Gatekeeper 萧注 was from Xin Yu. He became an orphan at a young age and lived alone with his younger brother伯英. One day, he fell ill and was in a critical condition, gradually becoming drowsy and confused, to the point where he could not be heard breathing.伯英 and the servants wept by his side. As evening approached, an old woman, thin and hunched, emerged from under the bathroom and slowly walked towards the bedroom, crying and saying, "Six郎, return my life." Six郎 was a nickname for萧注. As she was about to climb the steps, an old man in white robes, with a beard as white as snow, came out from the western side of the house, leaning on a cane, and shouted, "How dare a lowly dead body appear on its own! Six郎 has more than ten thousand strings of official treasury money, which he has never used. If he were to die, it is his fate to end, and he would still become the master of a cave in 茆山. You, foolish and ignorant ghost, do not linger here, or I will deal with you!" He continued to shout at her, and the old woman cried in sorrow, then crawled back to her original place. The old man also disappeared. By midnight,萧注 began to moan, drank some gruel, and recovered after several days.伯英 calmly described what he had seen, and萧注's face changed color, saying, "When you were not at home, the old maid did not serve me and spoke to me in abusive language. I hit her with an iron whip, and she died. I secretly buried her under the bathroom. Could the one you saw be her?"

A farmer from Le Ping, after returning from planting rice, was called out by someone and saw several figures outside, who had strange and ugly appearances. They ordered him to carry a load. Passing through several villages, including Hongyuan Shicun and He Heng, they visited the village gods at each village, stating their intention to spread pestilence, but were all refused and did not listen. The strange group said, "Then there is only Liu Village; we can go to Liu Shijiu's house!" They proceeded, directly entering and going to the various rooms under the eaves to stay, with no food, drink, bedding, or pillows. The next morning, Liu Shijiu's son came out, and the leader of the strange group told his followers, "Strike the right foot of this person." As soon as the stick hit, the child fell to the ground. Then an old woman passed by, and they ordered to strike her left foot; the old woman fell to the ground, and three people were harmed in succession. But they only stayed in one room and did not wander out. A spy secretly reported, "A tiger jumped forward from the front, and it is very fearsome." The leader's expression did not change, and he sent two ghosts to wait with sticks, saying that when they arrived, they would strike both feet. Soon after, the tiger was killed under the stick. After two days, the spy urgently reported, "There was a fire in the north." In a short while, the flames reached the house, and the water and mountains came in great force. The strange group looked around in panic and fear, and had no time to take their luggage, fleeing alone in a hurry. They were angry at the farmer for not helping, and pushed him into a ditch in the field. He suddenly got up, and then found himself lying in bed. His wife and children had been crying around him for three days. The villagers asked the Liu family about the matter, and they said that two sons and a maid were simultaneously suffering from an illness, and they called a witch to treat them, but they died at the door. They invited another witch, who was punished for the previous incident and wanted to catch the ghosts unawares. She performed magic from the back door, held a knife, blew a horn, recited a water and fire wheel spell, and entered. The sick people were all well the same day.

A Man from Zizhou Named Tian Gu Tian Gu, a man from Zizhou, had a niece who married the son of Liu Langzhong from You County. Liu had been deceased for several years, and Tian's family was now ill. Tian sent a servant to Hengshan to invite his cousin Zhang Minzhong, intending to entrust him with future affairs, but Zhang failed to come, and Tian did not recover. Initially, Tian had an older brother who married a woman from Hengshan named Liao. After the wife died, he married her younger sister. The brother also died, leaving only the younger sister-in-law. So, Tian went with Zhang Minzhong to pay respects and stayed at Zhang's old residence, the Moshan Pavilion, during the winter of the year Jia Shen in the Longxing era. That night, the younger sister-in-law suddenly had a heart attack and was slightly better after medical treatment. After midnight, she wanted to get up and sit, but she spoke incoherently. Zhang went to visit her, and her older sister appeared, scolding her younger sister sternly, saying, "Why must we share a husband? After death, no place was set up to worship me, making me have nowhere to rely on during festivals. It is not possible for us not to go together!" Zhang tried to reason with her, saying, "All of this is due to your brother-in-law. It is not your fault, sister-in-law. Since we are all sisters-in-law, how could we bear to do such a thing?" A little while later, she suddenly bowed her hands and said, "Uncle, may you have good fortune." She then said, "Qingsun, you can get on the bed and sit." Uncle Tian was the younger uncle of Tian's third aunt, and Qingsun was her young son, both of whom had died. It seemed that the room was filled with spirits, and everyone was terrified.

Soon after, the form changed to resemble the voice of the Tians, and she turned to Zhang and said, "Zhang, it is my aunt who has come. My aunt had something she wanted to say in life, and now I shall tell you everything." She invited him to come forward a little and described the time of her initial death, how her husband's elder brother took advantage of the maidservants, stole things, and the names and descriptions of the items, all exactly as they were. She also urged that the second son she raised be made heir to the Liu family, then spoke earnestly in a low voice, as if she did not want others to overhear. After a long while, she tearfully said, "I have committed no great sins, I have not fallen into the hell realm, but there is a schedule for rebirth, and I cannot immediately transcend." She sobbed and left. When it manifested, Liao's cheek dimples and slender fingers completely resembled Aunt Tians' appearance in life. She continued to come, until Liao returned. The following spring, when he was to be buried in the Liu family tomb, Zhang and Liao went to the funeral and stayed at the tomb site. As the cold rain fell heavily and the pine wind responded with a rustling sound, all were terrified, thinking that Liao had come again. Zhang rebuked her, saying, "It must be a wild spirit pretending. If it were truly Aunt Tians, why would her appearance not match that of last winter, changing with the voice, remaining unchanged. Repeatedly mentioned the thefts, urged and pleaded until satisfied." When Liao returned home, she came again to ask for a prayer from Zhang. A bystander said, "Zhang, the county magistrate lives nearby, why not go directly and tell him?" She said, "The dragon of the house is blocking me. Even if I want to enter, I am not allowed. I cannot avoid becoming a ghost." A year later, Liao died, and it finally ceased. Ghosts possessing living people is common, but only this one could make her appearance so similar, which is indeed strange.

Residents of Huangbai Road, South Gate, Fuzhou, Zhan Li and Zhan Qi, made a living by buying and selling silk and cloth. Their youngest son was called Xiao Ge. He once gambled and lost money, was afraid of being beaten and scolded by his elder brother, and ran away secretly to another place, not returning for a long time. The mother thought of him more deeply, and dreams and divinations were all inauspicious, so she believed he must have died. Coinciding with the Ghost Festival (Ullambana) and the offering of food to the hungry ghosts, the night before, the Zhan family spread paper money to await the offering. At dusk, there seemed to be a deep sigh from outside. The mother said, "Xiao Ge must have died, he is now coming to tell me." She took a piece of paper money and said, "If it is my son, can you pull out this paper money? If so, it will be a sign, and I will ask for your help from the underworld." After a while, there was a cold wind. Several times, they went in to check and pulled it out. The mother and elder brother cried out in shock, immediately called a monk to recite sutras to save his soul, and no longer hoped he would return. Several months later, he suddenly appeared from outside. The elder brother said, "He is a ghost." Took a knife to chase him away. The second brother quickly held him back and said, "Not yet." Approached to look closely, asked about his life and death. The younger brother said, "I originally ran away because I was afraid of being beaten, so I went to Yihuang to work for hire, I have not died." Thus it was known that the previous events were a trick by a ghost. Chao Duankui lived in the capital, liked a young woman in the neighborhood, cast glances and harbored feelings, but could not meet her. The woman suddenly came at night, took his clothes, and asked to share the bed. Chao was very happy. Before dawn, she left. He could not keep her. She said, "Like this, could it be that she is afraid her family knows?" After she left, there were traces of blood on the bed, and no one knew why. Three days later, passing by her door, he heard crying. Asked a neighbor, who said, "The young woman died in childbirth, it has been three days." Chao wiped away his tears and returned home.

In the year of Gaozong's reign, during the year of the White Earth Ox (1133), at the village ferry of Xincheng County, under the bright moonlight, a fisherman moored his boat to return home. He heard someone on the opposite bank calling for a boat to cross. He went to them and found more than twenty people with tattoos and carrying weapons. Thinking they were soldiers from a fort, he did not have time to ask questions and took them aboard. After they crossed, he took out money from his bag and gave it to them. They left the shore and departed. In the past, soldiers passing by never did this. The fisherman was both happy and surprised. The next day, he looked at the money and found it was all paper. He then realized they were ghosts. More than a hundred li east of Nancheng County, on Longmen Mountain, there is a temple on the mountain peak. It is secluded and quiet, rarely visited by people, and only one monk lives there. A visiting monk stayed in another room, far from the main monk's room. After sleeping, he heard voices outside the door and was startled and did not dare to get up. A moment later, the door creaked open by itself. The guest was very frightened and dared not breathe. He got off the bed and wanted to run away, but the door was already blocked by a huge stone. He shouted for a long time, and the main monk finally responded. Just as they were answering each other, the stone suddenly disappeared, and the door opened as before. The guest could no longer sleep and went to stay with the main monk. He also asked about the strange occurrence, and the monk said it was the work of mountain spirits. He had seen many such occurrences before, but they could not harm each other.

A Painter from Linchuan named Huang, while traveling to Guangchang, arrived at Zhijuba Zhai, where the military officer Yan hosted him. One midnight, a woman appeared under the lamp, quite attractive, and Huang, taking advantage of his drunkenness, flirted with her. She readily agreed, and when asked who she was, she said she was the wife of the household. From then on, she came every night. When Huang was in need, he would secretly provide her with money. They stayed together for half a year, and Huang gradually became weak and sickly. Yan asked him about it, but Huang refused to say. Initially, Yan often hid a prostitute, and his wife, unable to bear the anger and jealousy, hanged herself in her room. Even after burial, she continued to appear as a ghost, and the room was empty, with no one daring to live there. But Huang lived there, and Yan thought it was a ghost. When Yan told him the reason, Huang finally confessed the truth. Yan spat and cursed at the air, and Huang was forced to leave and stay at a hotel. That night, she came again, and Huang was planning to avoid her. The woman said, "There is no need to avoid me; I would not harm you. Even if you run away, I will come." Reluctantly, Huang stayed with her. As time went on, Huang began to fear for his own safety and rushed back to his hometown. Halfway, he rested and sought shade under a mulberry tree, where the woman came again. She said, "This thief is too heartless. We have been together for so long, without a single thought of attachment. Can you bear to abandon me? You should return quickly!" Huang dared not answer, only praying to heaven and earth and silently reciting scriptures. The woman suddenly sighed and said, "This is my fault. I should not have been misled, leading to today's situation with no future. Animals are not worth admiring. Can I not choose a different partner?" She left, and the strange occurrences ceased.

In the month of November in the year "庚午" (1140 AD) during the reign of Emperor Gaozong (Zhao Gou) of the Song dynasty, in the village of Yong'an in Xin Cheng County, Jiangxi Province, at midnight, hundreds or thousands of people walking were heard. Some were talking, some were laughing, some were singing, and some were crying. The scene was chaotic and hurried, with details not clearly understood. No one was not frightened or amazed by the event. The cold and dim conditions made even things close by indistinguishable. Those with courage opened doors to look closely but saw nothing. The next morning, the snow was more than a foot deep. In the snow, tracks resembling those of troops were found, with human, animal, and bird tracks mixed together. Some tracks were stained with blood. The phenomenon extended for several miles before disappearing into the deep mountains. In the year "癸酉" (1273 AD) during the reign of Emperor Duzong (Zhao Shi) of the Song dynasty, a man selling a jeweled hat passed by Longhua Temple in Jishui County. A woman came out to buy a jeweled hat, paying a thousand coins initially and agreeing to pay the rest the next day. She went back as scheduled, but the monk asked if there could be a woman in Longhua Temple. The man did not believe the claim. The monk ordered a search, which revealed that the woman's coffin was stored in the temple, with the hat on top and the thousand coins missing.

In the eastern region of Jiang, there was a man named Zhang Cuifu, who was upright, courageous, and unafraid of ghosts and spirits. During the reign of Emperor Shenzong in the Song dynasty, he resided in the prefectural school. Behind the school, there was a pavilion that was often haunted. After dusk, shadows and forms would appear, and no one dared to go there alone. His peers teased him, saying, "If you are not afraid of ghosts, place a zither in the pavilion and play it at dusk. We will all lean on tables and wait." Zhang agreed, and at midnight, he went to the pavilion and played several tunes. As he was about to return, he heard breathing sounds from the corner of the pavilion. The moon was dim and bright, and upon looking, he saw someone lying on their side. He touched from head to toe and found it to be a woman, her body warm and soft. He scolded her, but she did not speak. He then played another tune, returned to his study, and lit candles to examine her. She was clearly a young woman, lying dead. He poured water on her, and she revived, saying that Mr. Chen, the scholar-official, had invited her there. Mr. Chen, the scholar-official, had died more than a year ago. In the morning, he visited her parents, who said she had been sick for half a year, suffering from consumption, and they also suspected she was a ghost or spirit. He took her home, gave her strong medicine, and she recovered. In Yongxin Prefecture, there was a doctor named Lin Xingke. In the year Dingyou of the Dade era, one evening, an old woman came to his door and invited him to go to the west gate to treat a patient. Thinking it was late, Lin asked the woman to leave for the next morning. In the morning, he prepared the medicine. The woman urged him to go, and about five miles away, they arrived at the front of the East Peak Temple. She said, "Wait here." Under the moonlight, Lin saw the woman enter a tomb and disappear. He was surprised by this and climbed to the temple pavilion, closed the door, and peeked through the window crack. He saw the woman lead a tiger to the spot. There was no one around. She patted the tiger's back and said, "Too bad!" She then cursed, "For three years, I planned this piece of meat for you, but your share is so small!" At dawn, Lin called for villagers to take her back. Since then, he has not dared to go out.

In the year 1274 during the Yuan Dynasty, Shangguan Shiping went to the Confucian temple in Jishui to accompany in worship. The night before, he arrived at a high house in the county, where he was accompanied by two others and two servants, all staying in a small tower. That night, they suddenly disappeared. The companions went out, and the door was still locked as it was. After a thorough search, they were not found, and the host was greatly frightened, wanting to report to the authorities. Shangguan Shiping, along with his fellow townsman and Confucian scholar Li Cangshan, was sent back. Shangguan Shiping said that in the middle of the night, while in a daze, three people pulled him along to return, breaking through the door's gap. They reached a place where they had to cross water, and he just remembered that he had come specifically to worship at the temple, but he was afraid of the water and dared not cross, so he stopped and did not move. The three people then pulled him across, but Shangguan Shiping forcefully tried to pull back, and he just realized that he was being haunted. He then recited the spell of the Supreme Emperor, and the three people pulled him around the land, but whenever they reached water, they would forcefully pull back. One person crossed the water first and returned shortly, touching his face and saying, "You recited the spell of the Supreme Emperor, but why did you miss two words?" Shangguan Shiping continued to recite without stopping. Not long after, he heard the sound of the bell, and the three people finally dispersed.
