During the Xianchun period of the Song Dynasty, Fu Qinke stayed at Zhang Jiyou's inn in Shantian, Duchang County. Every night, two maids came with candles and tea bottles, carrying a silver steel lacquer tray with two sugar cakes, and provided attentive service. This continued for more than two months, even in bad weather. Once, when the host was free, he asked why the tea and cakes served every night were so good and where they came from. Zhang Jiyou was puzzled and didn't understand. Fu Qinke explained the reason, and Zhang Jiyou warned him not to reveal it, and to secretly observe the changes. On that night, Fu Qinke drank the tea and kept the tray, only giving it back after being firmly asked. In the morning, he showed the host, who found a character "于" on the bottom of the tray. No one knew its origin, but the cakes were sold at Shantian Temple. When asked the cake seller, they said many had been lost before, and the women also did not come again. After the Yuan Dynasty's submission, Hu Yuanyan was traveling for studies in the region of Lake Xiang and Yong. He stayed overnight at an open temple, where an old monk said while facing away from the light, "Why did the guest arrive so late?" Hu replied, "The inn was hard to find, and traveling was very difficult." The monk took out two vegetarian meals from his sleeve and lay down on the ground. The monk recited a verse: "A patched robe and an old bowl, for several years, I have lived in this mountain with me. A cold lamp illuminates the lotus seat at night, and I can accompany me while adding a few more pages of scripture." In the morning, Hu felt his clothes were wet and cold, and upon touching the mat, he realized he had slept on the grass. The sky was blue, and the wilderness was empty, but the temple was clearly the place where he had stayed the night. He was surprised and told the monk about it, who said this was the burial place of the ancestor. The ancestor had passed away fifteen years ago, and at night, five vegetarian meals were offered, but two were missing.

In Tan Prefecture, there was a place of serene enlightenment. During the reign of Emperor Lizong of Song (1241-1264), a wandering scholar named Hu Tianjun stayed there. Under the moonlight, he played the qin (a traditional Chinese zither) under a plum tree and saw a beautiful woman who hesitated to approach. Hu played three pieces with intent, and the woman slowly approached. Hu greeted her respectfully, and she said, "Although my voice is harmonious and sorrowful, I have many desires that cannot be fulfilled directly." Hu took her hand and said, "In this world, there are no true kindred spirits; meeting tonight, is it not a genuine karmic connection?" The woman bowed and left, saying, "On the next clear night, I will come to meet you." The next day, friends dragged him into the city, and he drank and played for two nights, forgetting to return. He greatly regretted missing the appointment and hurried back. Under the tree, he found a white silk handkerchief with a poem written on it. The poem read: "The wind blows gently, the moonlight slants, heavy dew presses against the cloud rings, and the jade ornaments are weighed down. She stands gazing at the distant clouds for a long time, her hands trembling as she wipes away tears from the plum blossoms." Hu was melancholic and went to sleep. The next day, he showed the handkerchief to others, and Zhao Binghu was shocked and said, "My deceased wife, Qiao Shi from Hangzhou, named Wang Xian, was the niece of a noble lady. She died suddenly last year and was buried behind the plum tree. This is her handwriting." He poured wine as an offering and composed a poem: "The young man regrets missing the good time, drinking in Changsha at night and accidentally forgetting to return. She must be thinking about her fragrant soul stepping on the waning moon, her clothes soaked with rolling dew as she left."

During the reign of Emperor Lizong in the Southern Song dynasty, a man named Yuan Zibo from Gaoyou Commandery was traveling at night through the winding mountain pass of Jiukuqu in Anqing Prefecture. He became lost and did not know the way, and saw a thatched cottage with a firelight. Inside, two scholars were burning charcoal and sitting opposite each other, reading books. They invited him to sit near the fire and talked and laughed naturally. One of them said, "I saw a poem by Zhang Zongyong yesterday that says, 'Great writings must guard against aging, and buying more farmland to prepare for leisure.' The meaning of these two lines is also good." The other scholar replied, "Being human is also restricted. In my humble opinion, once old, one does not need to write, and if one has leisure, why must there be farmland?" The first scholar then quoted a poem by Ouyang Xiu, saying, "Writings are useless like painting tigers, and fame passes by like a fly. What do you think of this?" The second scholar responded, "That is truly the final word." Yuan Zibo, who throughout his life had been distant from rituals and ceremonies and lacked interest in acquiring farmland, heard the first two lines and found them to be in harmony with his heart. He then asked the names of the two scholars. The scholars laughed heartily while patting the table, but disappeared without a trace, and were found under a rock. The next morning, Yuan Zibo finally reached the correct path. The following day, he obtained a scroll of a tiger painted in Xuanchou, which he displayed on the back of a door panel and exposed to the sun. Flies gathered on it, and when he wiped it with his sleeve, the door panel fell to the ground, crushing a neighbor's child. This led to legal proceedings, and Yuan Zibo died in prison.

During the Song dynasty, there was a courtesan named Yan Chuchu from Huai. She accompanied a salt merchant named Lu Xinggan who was sailing on the Si River. One night, Lu Xinggan left the boat to attend to other matters, and Yan Chuchu, leaning against the railing, began to sing the "Hou Ting Hua" (Later Palace Flower) tune composed by Emperor Chen Shubao of the Southern Chen dynasty. Before she finished, a woman on the shore clapped her hands and recited: "Smoke veils the cold river, night veils the sand; At night, I dock near a wine house on the Qinhuai River. The courtesans do not know the sorrow of a fallen nation, Yet they still sing 'Hou Ting Hua' across the river." The woman sighed deeply and disappeared into the forest. The next day, when Lu Xinggan returned, he told Yan Chuchu about the incident. Yan Chuchu then hid by the riverside and sang the same tune again. The woman on the shore clapped her hands and recited the poem as before. The woman pursued Yan Chuchu into the forest and then vanished. The following day, they discovered a new grave. Upon inquiring, they learned that it was the burial site of Lady Pei, the wife of a general's soldiers. The general had a concubine whom he threw into the water. He then killed himself in the forest and was buried there.

In the year of Bingzi during the reign of Emperor Zhiyuan, a group of people named Luo from Yinguang,庐陵, were walking at night. They reached a place called Xijia Lake, where they ate salted plums. They placed the pits in the mouth of a skull by the roadside and asked, "Is it salty?" As they continued walking, they came to a place called Changkeng, where the moonlight was bright. They saw a dark, rotating mass following them, and it shouted, "Salty!" The group was greatly frightened and walked quickly for more than ten miles until they reached Rongcun and crossed the water, where they finally stopped hearing the noise. During the Song dynasty, in the eastern part of Lushao Market in Yuanzhou, there was a silversmith named Guo, who was in his thirties and lived alone. In the west of the market, there was an old woman who often visited Guo to buy and sell jewelry. One night, her daughter, who was fifteen or sixteen years old, ran to Guo and said, "I wish to be your concubine!" Guo was startled and asked why. The girl replied, "I have admired you for a long time and recently found a way to escape. Please do not doubt me." When Guo asked for the details, the girl said, "I pretended to die, and my mother placed me in a coffin. I opened the coffin and came out, then covered it again. My mother buried the empty coffin and did not look for me anymore." Guo placed her in a secret room and did not allow her to come out. After a month, the girl's mother accidentally found out that Guo was dead and peeked into his room, seeing the red shoes the girl had buried. She took the shoes and called out to the neighbors, saying, "Guo stole and opened my daughter's grave!" When Guo returned, the neighbors told him what had happened, and he was greatly alarmed.

The woman said, "Mother has suddenly arrived; quickly avoid her, for I have forgotten to pick up my shoes. I will temporarily avoid her; you have nothing to worry about." After she left, Guo fled to Tan Prefecture, traveling more than ten miles early in the morning. The woman followed him and they both arrived in Tan Prefecture. After a long time, their money ran out. The woman said, "I am good at singing palace tunes; there should be someone who appreciates my voice." So she began performing in the Pingli Ward, and her singing was so beautiful that it seemed to stop the clouds. The audience was so dense that it formed a wall, and she earned hundreds of tickets daily. Wealthy families competed to invite her, throwing gold hairpins as rewards. In less than a year, she had accumulated ten thousand coins. One day, a Daoist hermit with a distinctive hairstyle, nine feet tall, patted Guo's back and said, "Thousands of people are watching this ghost puppet!" Guo realized the truth and led the hermit to a secluded place, bowing to him and asking for salvation. The hermit instructed him to pray at the Temple of the Eastern Emperor. Guo went to the temple and prayed. By the second watch, he saw a woman in chains rushing to the rear palace of the Eastern Emperor. Suddenly, she fell to the ground, and it turned out to be a dead body. Guo then realized that the ghost had possessed the woman's body. He spent all his resources to rebuild the temple to redeem the woman's sins, and respectfully burned and buried her with great ceremony. That night, he dreamed that the woman thanked him and cried as she left.

In the market area of Qilishi, Jishui, there was a butcher named Wangyang who made his living by slaughtering animals. During the Duanping era, a friend of his once went out early in the morning. Before reaching Wang's residence by two or three miles, the area was somewhat desolate, and it was said to be haunted. His heart occasionally felt doubt and fear. Suddenly, someone called out to him to walk together, saying, "I am tired, would you like to take turns carrying me?" The friend replied, "Good, I will carry you to a certain place, and then you will carry me to another place." By the time they reached a certain place, it was just starting to get light. Turning around, he saw that the person's hands had hair, and when he touched them, they were indeed hairy. The friend asked to be put down but was refused, and so was carried without being let go. As they approached Wang's residence, he carefully took the person's hand and put it down, only to find it was a sheep. The friend untied the sheep, took it to Wang's residence, and knocked on the door, saying to Wang, "I owe the government money and only have one sheep, would you like to buy it for four thousand?" Wang examined the sheep and said, "It's worth only three thousand." The friend replied, "But I need the money urgently, please give me the cash!" Wang handed over three thousand in cash, and the friend left the sheep and the rope, saying to Wang, "The sheep may wander a bit, please keep it safe." He then took the money and went to the city. When he returned and passed by Wang's place, only the rope was left. People then called it the Wang Sheep.

**Final Translation:** During the reign of Emperor Lizong in the Song Dynasty, Peng Danxuan, a man from Sichuan, resigned from his position as deputy governor of Jiangdong and traveled to Mount Wuyi. Once, while wandering alone through the forests and reeds, he entered a thatched hut and saw two scholars dressed in formal attire eating together. They invited Peng to sit beside them. On the tray were one pig head, one sheep lung, and one chicken. The person sitting on the right said, "Pouring wine on the ground is called a sacrifice, and today people call it a memorial offering. It is placed in its proper place, not poured on the ground. Sacrifice and feast are different; the gods are called to enjoy, taking the essence to reach the heavens, while sacrifice is merely pouring wine on the ground!" The person sitting on the left said, "Food is placed to the left of the person, and lamb to the right, for convenience in eating and drinking." The person on the right continued, "Ancient people took the meaning of yin and yang in every meal, eating six grains, the earth's produce, to create yang virtue, therefore it is placed to the left."

Ginger soup is primarily made with six kinds of animals, a product of heaven, and serves to create merit in the afterlife, which is why it is placed on the right. After finishing eating, the sacrificial table was removed, and Peng asked the person on the right for their surname and courtesy name. They replied that their surname was Wei. When asked for their courtesy name, they said they were an old man of the mountains and forests, with no courtesy name to offer. Peng then asked the person on the left for their surname and courtesy name, but they bowed respectfully without speaking. Peng engaged in conversation with the person on the right about ancient and modern matters, as well as the *Tian Xuan Tu Yi* (先天图易), *Chuan* (传), and *Xing Li* (性理) studies, all of which were mysterious and profound, beyond understanding. Seeing that the day was ending, Peng took his leave, and the two escorted him to the door before departing. Peng heard the person on the right say, "The Han system was completely ruined by Emperor Wu, and the Tang system was completely ruined by Emperor Ming Huang." The next day, Peng brought his servant and a jug of wine, but there was no path to reach the place. He descended the mountain to a wealthy family and explained the situation. The wealthy family said, "Strange, yesterday we went to the Temple of Zhu Gong to offer sacrifices, and the dishes we ate were exactly the same." Peng then realized that the person on the left was Wen Gong, and the person on the right was Wei Heshan, as everything they said was recorded in the *Heshan Ya Yan* (鹤山雅言).

A man named Yang Er Lang from Jiankang engaged in trade with the South Seas, traveling back and forth for over ten years and accumulating wealth in the tens of millions. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (Chunxi era), he encountered bandits on the waves, and all his companions were killed. Yang fell into the water first and managed to survive. He found a piece of wood, hugged it, and floated up and down for two days before drifting to an island. He left the wood and climbed ashore, walking confidently into a cave. Inside, men and women gathered to watch him, many of whom were naked, but their voices could be distinguished. A woman, known as the Ghost Mother, had a large entourage of attendants. She exclaimed in surprise, "There seems to be a living person here!" She sent a young girl servant to investigate, who saw Yang Er Lang and ran to report to the Ghost Mother, who ordered her to bring Yang forward. The Ghost Mother asked, "Do you wish to stay here?" Yang, thinking there was no way to escape, reluctantly entrusted his fate and answered, "I wish to stay." The Ghost Mother instructed the girl servant to prepare a room for him and made them husband and wife. About two years later, his diet, daily life, and habits were the same as those in the world. Once, a messenger came with a letter saying that a true immortal had invited the Ghost Mother to visit a celestial abode. From then on, every ten days or once a month, she would go, and all her attendants followed, leaving Yang alone in the cave.

One day, he respectfully asked to accompany her on her journey. His mother said, "You are just an ordinary person, how can you go there?" He repeatedly made earnest requests, and she suddenly granted his permission. He walked lightly as if stepping on clouds, arriving at a hall. The feast was abundant and delicious. Those who arrived took their seats. The ghost mother told Xiao Yang to hide under the table and remain silent and still. After the feast, they burned paper money, and then heard crying. Upon closer listening, it was his wife, children, and relatives. Xiao Yang came out from under the table and called his family, but they all shouted at him in anger, thinking he was a ghost. Only his wife wept and said, "You were thrown into the sea, and we have heard nothing from you. We held a funeral, observed mourning, performed a soul-summoning ceremony, and had a burial divination. Tonight, we are dismissing the spirit, so we have established a water and land Buddhist service to benefit you. How could you be here? Is it perhaps another strong spirit possessing you?" Xiao Yang said, "I am truly a person!" He explained the reason, and the family finally believed him. The ghost mother tried to call him but he wouldn't go, and she then shouted at him angrily, but she couldn't get close to him, and soon after, she left. Xiao Yang's family called a doctor and treated him with medicine, and after several years, his appearance returned to normal. By the middle of the Song Dynasty, he was still alive.

In Jiankang, Huang Xifu formed a marital alliance with Zeng Hongzi from Jinxi, Fuzhou. Huang was a quiet and reserved person, and whenever he visited Zeng, he always stayed at Zeng's place. Huang's residence was next to a monastery, and the monks would clean a government room for his use. During the Shaochun era, Huang's mother passed away, and he went to Zeng to thank him for his hospitality. Afterward, he returned to the monastery, where the monks put him in a separate room. Huang felt that the monks were undervaluing him and became slightly unhappy. That night, under the bright moon, he went outside to cool off and saw the government room lit up with candles, with a lively conversation taking place inside. Suddenly, a gentleman greeted him and said, "Are you not Huang Xifu? I dare to invite you for tea." Huang asked, "Who are you?" The gentleman replied, "I am Zhang Weiji, a low-ranking official who has come to this area." He then invited Huang to join him for a drink and introduced his wife and daughter. Zhang said, "I have a daughter who is still young, and I believe she is a good match for you. Would you be willing to marry her?" Huang replied, "I am still in mourning for my mother and dare not consider such matters." Zhang argued, "A man of learning like yourself should not be bound by strict rules, just as a louse in a pair of pants cannot be bothered by its position." He then brought Huang into the room and forcibly performed a wedding ceremony, following the rituals of marriage. Huang felt conflicted, realizing that he had violated the norms of society, and became disoriented.

Suddenly, Huang heard the sound of a gong being struck. He then realized that he had been deceived by a supernatural force. He tried to get up, but a woman in the room held him back. Unable to resist, he rushed out and loudly called for his servant. Wei Ji also came out and called out, saying, "Why are you treating me like a ghost, Xie Fu?" He tried to hold Huang back. The monk got up, lit a lamp, and escorted Huang to Zeng. Huang still saw Wei Ji and his wife and daughters following him. Huang asked, "Who was it that struck the gong?" The others replied, "It was the rooster crowing." Huang became even more certain of his realization. By morning, Zeng escorted him back home. Wei Ji's wife and daughters followed, and Huang moved, they moved; Huang stopped, they stopped. Others did not see them, only Huang saw them. He fell gravely ill upon arriving home. Secretary Zhang Yuanwei, who was close to Huang, came to visit his illness. Before reaching two li, Huang said, "Secretary Zhang is coming." Soon enough, Zhang arrived. When asked, it turned out that it was Wei Ji who had informed him. Huang was ill for several months before recovering. Initially, Huang was drinking, and his servant found it strange that he had not returned. The servant went to the official quarters and was told that Huang was still there drinking. The servant returned and went to sleep first. Later, the servant asked the monk, who said that there was a guest named Zhang Wei Ji who had brought his family to stay at the temple. Due to an epidemic, his wife and daughters had died in the official quarters.

**Record of Strange Events, Volume 2** When Governor Ge Wenkang was serving as the Minister of Education, there was a stable hand suffering from leprosy, whose face was covered with sores and was quite ugly. Because he was not assigned to other tasks, the condition was not addressed immediately. One day, the stable hand drove the horse to the Taoist temple, stopping at the gate. Governor Ge entered the temple to burn incense and, upon exiting, noticed that the stable hand's face was clean and free of blemishes. He was surprised and asked the stable hand about it. The stable hand did not know what had happened and said that he had seen a Taoist priest come and play with him, touching his face and then leaving. Governor Ge sent a subordinate to search for the Taoist priest, but the priest was no longer there. Another Taoist priest named Liu Jingzhen was performing a Taoist ritual and passing by a nobleman's house, taking a short rest outside. At midnight, the moon was bright, and he saw a figure dressed in green and black, with a hanging headscarf, leading a group of several attendants. The figure pointed to the nobleman's house and said, "You never offered me wine as a sacrifice, and the candlestick fell, burning my face!" Liu Jingzhen looked closely and realized that the figures were ghosts. The next morning, Liu Jingzhen visited the nobleman to inquire about the incident. The nobleman explained that he had invited several guests to drink and that the candlestick had fallen unexpectedly, causing the priest's face to be burned.

An Elderly Man Named Zhu from Qingzhou An elderly man named Zhu from Qingzhou supported himself by selling medicine. He often traveled with his wife, concubine, and dog, moving back and forth between Ganzhou and Nankang County, and was well-known to the locals. In the fourth month of the Dingchou year of the Shaoxing era (1137 AD), Zhu returned to Nankang and arrived at his inn. A villager from Huanggang in southern Ganzhou came to him, claiming that his mother was ill and inviting Zhu to visit his home for a diagnosis. When asked how far it was, the villager replied that it was several miles. Zhu immediately followed. Upon arrival, Zhu took all their money and belongings, killed three people, and buried them in the woods. The dog followed and barked. Soon after, Zhu left, and the villager pursued him relentlessly, directly returning to his old residence in Nankang County. There, Zhu scratched the ground with his claws and mourned incessantly. The residents of the inn were surprised and led him to the county. The dog crouched in the courtyard, as if making a complaint. The county magistrate said to him, "Is your master not killed by thieves? I will send archers to capture them." The dog immediately stood up, wagged its tail, and led the group to the burial site. They dug up the grave and found the bodies. The archers then said to him, "Although the bodies have been found, lead us to the bandits' house." The dog led the way again, and they captured all the criminals. --- A Man from Putian Yeh Yuanhuan, the nephew of Chancellor Yeh Subo, was a scholar from Zhaozhou and served as a judge in Huizhou. One day, a wet nurse went out with her child to play and saw a man dressed in red holding a stick, measuring the ground. He happened to come near her side and drew a line, saying, "This is good!" Then he left. The wet nurse was surprised that there was no one like this in the city, and she returned to tell Yeh. Yeh called officials to search for the man, but they found nothing. The next day, a fire broke out in the city, burning many houses and buildings, and it stopped at the hall of the judge.

During the thirteenth year of the Chongxi era, Wanqi Yanzhong served as the governor of Wugang. He had a daughter who was around ten years old and had not yet married. She suddenly fell ill with a disoriented and confused condition, unable to describe what she had encountered, but was languid and weak as if in a drunken or dream-like state. Various religious and magical practices, including exorcisms and divinations, were tried, but none were effective. Her mother, Zhao, was particularly concerned about her condition. Over time, the illness worsened. Her birthday was in June, and on that day, Yanzhong believed that the evil star's influence would diminish. As the family gathered in the hall to drink wine, a small tray slowly descended from the air and landed on the ground. Everyone rushed to see it, and it turned out to be an old lacquered tray containing a clay pot, a clay cup, three painted plates, and stored lychees and longans. However, the contents of the pot were filth and urine. There was also a small piece of paper note, which read: "Encouraging Lady Six to drink to her longevity." Yanzhong was displeased and called all his officials to recount the strange and unusual events, saying, "As the chief of the people, I have been subjected to such vile and contemptuous treatment. This is a great failure in my duties!" He intended to submit a petition to resign from his position and return to Jun, but his colleagues advised against it, and he stopped. After about ten days, the transport commissioner Lin Huangzhong submitted a report, leading to Yanzhong's dismissal. His replacement, Li Dazhong, came to investigate the cause, and it was said that a flood had occurred in the city park, where there was an old temple that had been abandoned and demolished for fifty years, and it was believed to be causing the trouble. Yanzhong had indeed always wanted to rebuild it but had not succeeded. Thus, they rebuilt the temple at that location, and the strange occurrences ceased. At the time, Cao Jingzhi, serving as the deputy magistrate of Dong'an County in Yongzhou, personally witnessed these events and the tray and pot, according to his account (Cao Jingzhi's statement).

The Deity of the Shao Wu Huiying Temple, initially bestowed the title of Yuemin Gong, had a shrine established in Jiangtun Village, Jianyang, during the first year of the Jianzhongjingguo era. Scholar Jiang Yan paid homage at the shrine and that night dreamed of going to the southern shrine of the gods. He heard singing, and the gatekeeper stopped him and said, "You and your lady are sitting under the white cloud screen, adjusting and composing new lyrics; do not rush in!" Shortly after, the deity summoned Jiang Yan and asked, "Have you heard this song?" Jiang Yan, filled with fear, replied, "How could such a thing be heard in the world?" The deity said, "This is a melody of the Huangzhong Gong with a silk-wrapped ballad." It then recited the lyrics: "Bent new embankment, dominating the village with auspicious atmosphere. Painted eaves stretching to the clouds, mountains and waters intertwine in a green tapestry, towers by the shore, golden and green, leaning against each other in the distance, willow shadows low, reflecting the brilliance of flowers. Who first brought about this peaceful prosperity? Generally, the scenery is shaped by people; what was once a desolate fortress is now a place of incense and worship." Jiang Yan woke up in shock and immediately recorded and spread the lyrics, but no one could sing it. Attendant Cao Yanheng lived in Yangzhou with his wife Lady Yang. One day, while gathered with their children in the hall, a great thunderstorm suddenly erupted. A strange ghost fell from the sky to the ground, about three feet tall, with a blue face and flesh. On its head was a hat like the world's headscarf, made of flesh and connected to its forehead. It looked at people, covering its face as if smiling. Soon, the number of observers gradually increased, and the laughter also did not stop. Soon, a great thunderstorm struck the roof, clouds and haze obscured everything, and it was impossible to distinguish people. Suddenly, it flew away into the sky.

The Judicial Officer of Yangzhou, Mr. Shen (whose name and hometown are unknown), was known for his upright and strict character. Deputy Governor Rao Huaiqing had a particularly close relationship with him. When Rao Huaiqing returned to Linchuan after being relieved of his duties, the officials of the prefecture bid him farewell at Guazhou. The night before, Shen heard a voice from outside his window saying, "Your fortune will end and your horse will fail tomorrow!" He told his wife and children, who were deeply troubled by this omen. The next day, as he was about to mount his horse, his son pulled at his clothes to stop him. Shen replied, "I have a close friendship with Deputy Governor Rao, and we are parting after a long journey; how could I not see him off?" He rode off directly, on a horse that he had borrowed from the military, which was in very poor condition. As soon as he left the city, the horse galloped and fell, with Shen's foot getting stuck in the stirrup and unable to be freed. He galloped for forty miles before finally stopping at Guazhou. The stable master caught up with him, finding him with his face and body covered in blood and flesh, making him unrecognizable. They carried him back to his house, where he breathed weakly and died the next day. Rao Huaiqing, feeling sorry for Shen's death, gave twenty thousand coins as a funeral gift. The prefecture sent more than ten laborers to escort the coffin back home. On the way, the other officials looked at each other, as if they were covered in ice and frost. If they were a little lazy, their heads would ache, as if something were hitting them. Those walking by also saw an official in green robes sitting on the coffin, holding a stick and looking around. This continued until they reached home. More than a year later, Shen's wife, Lady Yan, saw flags moving slowly through the air during the day. A person riding a white horse slowly descended, and it turned out to be Shen. He comforted and patted her for a long time, then called out to all the sons, teaching them about reading and farming. He said, "I am now a Ghostly Official, with a powerful and prosperous career. Do not forget me." He flew away gracefully, and did not come again. Lady Yan's guests spread the story.

Teng Boxiu served as the governor of Xuanzhou. During his leisure time from governance, he often lectured on the *Yi Jing* at the Yibai Hall, where guests and listeners were often a dozen or so. After one lecture, when the guests had departed, he sat alone on his meditation mat. Suddenly, he saw a person in red clothing bow before him and say, "Please come to the hall to see the snow." It was the end of July, and the summer wind was still strong. Teng Boxiu found this strange, doubting that snow could fall at this time of year, and wondering how he, a minor official, could have a red-clothed official reporting to him. Reluctantly standing up, he had just taken a few steps when a loud sound like thunder erupted. The beam of the hall had already broken, and the meditation mat was crushed to pieces.

The Temple of Dongyue in Zizhou was located within the city walls, consisting of only a small hall, two wings, and a gate tower. The local people often complained about its small size. In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era of the Southern Song Dynasty, a passerby visited the temple and was possessed by a supernatural entity, which instructed them to construct a grand temple, setting a very strict deadline. Onlookers gathered around the walls, all showing deep respect and belief. The next day, within a hundred miles of the prefecture, all large trees at the foot of the mountains were marked for the temple. People from far and near worked together to contribute. Soon, a grand temple was completed, built on a hill and expanded upward, becoming the most impressive building in the region. All prayers made with sincerity were answered immediately. Those who filed complaints based on grievances died within a few days, except for those who were innocent, who remained calm and recovered after a night. They described what they saw in the underworld, which was entirely like the world, except for the presence of the Bull-Headed Judge and fierce beasts whose breath burned the sinners. People outside the temple often heard the sounds of beating at night. In the twenty-first year of the Southern Song Dynasty, the prefecture governor, Left Shoudao from Poyang, arrived in office. He found the temple's influence excessive and posted a notice prohibiting people from filing complaints, locking the temple doors. From then on, all previous lawsuits and complaints ceased.

In the spring of the fourth year of the Chunxi era, the county magistrate of Poyang, Wu Zhengguo, dreamed of arriving at the underworld, which appeared like a shrine. Two gatekeepers greeted him with hands clasped in a gesture of respect, and as he was bowing and greeting them, he suddenly woke up. He told the guest at the inn, Jie Chun Nian, about the dream, and Jie found it very ominous. Jie said, "The county magistrate is the chief of the earth deity, and the deities should be respected." A few days later, in the morning, Wu was hearing cases when the prefect sent officials to urge payment of the spring clothing tax very urgently. Wu had no time to eat, climbed into his carriage, and hurried out to seek a loan from the mining and smelting official. He then went to the prefecture and felt slightly unwell, so he hastily returned home. In the carriage, he repeatedly tapped the crossbar to urge the driver to go faster. Within a hundred steps of the city, he could no longer bear it, so he ordered to visit a civilian's home and took a short rest. Just as he arrived at the Wu Tang Li Temple, he was helped inside. Family members rushed to see him, and he was already lying on a trundle bed, unable to speak. He died shortly thereafter. The two officials in the temple were exactly the ones seen in the dream. Wu was from Luoyang, and his governance was benevolent and beloved, so the people still miss him.

The two dragons of Datong Prefecture are very famous for their divine power, and the temple dedicated to them is especially majestic. Whenever the prefecture governor arrives, he must always repair and tidy it. They often transform into green snakes, and people call them Big Green and Little Green. Little Green is extremely fierce and violent, and people cannot provoke it. During the Zhenhe period, Li Youpu became the prefecture governor. After arriving, he refused to pay respects. His subordinates all requested, but he did not respond. While attending to official business during the day, a thunderclap sounded, and smoke and mist filled the air, making everything dark and unclear. It was impossible to see even a short distance away. There was a small hole in the box and the cover, both of which were pierced. When opened, the seal had already disappeared. Li did not know what to do. He searched for ten days, but did not find it. Everyone thought that the thunder without clouds must have been caused by a divine object. They knew that the dragon must be furious. They strongly requested to go and pray to it. Li had no choice but to go to the temple. He burned incense and humbly begged, and also apologized for his incompetence. While he was bowing and kneeling, thunder came out from the divine tent, directly reaching the place of the seal box and then stopping. When the box was opened, the seal was there as it was originally. From then on, Li dared not be disrespectful.

Han Yuanying of Yingchang, courtesy name Qinpu, served late in his career in the Jin Dynasty as the Director of Transportation for the regions around Bianliang and Luoyang. He had always been devout in his worship of the Lord of Mount Tai, and the deity eventually descended to his home. When the deity was about to arrive, he prepared a grand room, burned incense, and stood respectfully waiting. Soon after, the deity came solemnly, and there was a verbal exchange with someone present. A year later, the deity refused to appear again. Someone informed the local official Xin Jun, saying, "Governor Han is about to die!" When asked why, it was said that the deity had abandoned him, and why should he not die? Han Yuanying had been friends with Xin Jun, and he informed him of the matter, expressing his concern. He quickly dispatched a trusted servant with incense to Mount Tai to pray and offer thanks, instructing him, "The Holy Emperor only accepts the first offering of incense. Every morning, when the temple is opened for worship, the temple official is the one who presents the offering. If you follow him, the deity will surely respond. But if you delay even a moment, you will be carried up the mountain by a gust of wind, and even if you try to plead further, it will be too late. You should bribe the temple officials the day before, at dusk, to let you stay overnight. Wait until dawn to pray, or you will surely fail in your mission." The servant accepted the instructions and left. Upon entering the temple, he rested under the "Heaven-Piercing Drum" and, after a long journey, fell asleep from exhaustion. When he woke up, the main gate was already open. He saw a grand procession of attendants and guards, which he initially thought was the temple official returning, but realized it was unlike any usual scene. Then, the Holy Emperor emerged in a carriage, heading directly to the Inquiry Hall in the eastern temple. Han Yuanying was bound and followed behind, glancing back at the servant and continuing eastward. The servant realized he had failed in his task but still burned incense. After completing the ritual, he returned to report, falsely claiming to have followed the instructions. Han Yuanying scolded him, saying, "You were sleeping under the drum, and I saw you. How could you lie to me?" Within a month, Han Yuanying passed away. This account was told by Xin Qiji.

A Family from Nancheng The Yang family of Nancheng was quite wealthy. The eldest son, however, was unworthy and was expelled by his father. On a cold night, with nowhere to go, he entered a barn filled with hay and lay down on the grass to sleep. On a bright moonlit night, feeling lonely and cold, he could not sleep. Suddenly, a tiger leaped in, followed by several ghosts, all of which were spirits of those killed by tigers. The tiger and its ghosts headed directly towards the barn, took the grass and danced around, playing, while the son dared not breathe. Soon, dark clouds and strong winds appeared, dimming the surroundings. The tiger seemed to be chased and ran away in panic. The ghosts soon dispersed. Then, a divine being summoned and arrived, ordering the land god to be called. An old man came out to pay respects. The divine being reprimanded him, saying, "You have received the sacrifices of the Yang family for many years, yet you allowed the tiger to act violently. The young master almost got eaten, leading me to send divine soldiers to drive it away. You can truly be called derelict in your duty! I am the stove god and guardian spirit of their household. Do you recognize me?" The land god apologized and withdrew. The next morning, upon inspection, there were tiger tracks outside, and the grass was scattered on the ground. Later, the father's anger subsided, and the son was allowed to return, telling the whole story. From then on, he served the stove god more diligently.

During the reign of Emperor Ningzong of the Song dynasty, in Xingan County, there was an object struck by lightning that was touched by a pregnant woman and could not ascend back to the heavens. At that time, Zou the shaman could recite a purification spell. A deity appeared and said, "Recite the purification spell a thousand times, and there will be a reward." Zou recited the spell a thousand times, and the deity bestowed upon him a whip and an emblem, saying, "Use the emblem normally to pray for clear weather, and upside down to pray for rain. Use the whip to draw in the air, and the rain will stop where the drawing is made." The deity then suddenly disappeared, and the thunder ascended to heaven. Throughout his life, Zou enjoyed eating duck. Once, he visited his daughter and asked to have ducks killed and prepared as a meal. At the time, his daughter was drying lotus seeds in the courtyard, and the sky showed signs of rain. She wanted to gather the lotus seeds before serving them, saying that the rain would not harm them. Soon, there was heavy rain. Zou looked up into the sky and drew in all directions, and only the courtyard remained dry. From then on, people from far and near invited him for prayers. When the emblem was used according to the method, all requests were immediately fulfilled. He died at the age of over ninety, and the whip and emblem also disappeared. Now, the locals worship him in a temple at the front of Chengtian Palace on Yugu Mountain, simply calling him "Master Zou." Cui Fuzi, a man from Sichuan, lived in Fujian. His family had served in official positions for three generations, and his father reached the position of governor. Cui Fuzi inherited the position of Chengwu Lang. He served as an official in a certain place but was a wanderer and lacked discipline, especially enjoying gambling. During the Jiaxi era, his father, angry, expelled him. He stayed overnight in a temple in the village and could not sleep in the middle of the night. He heard a report saying, "The God of Zitong has arrived!" The temple deity came out and respected the God of Zitong sitting in the center. They spoke and responded, but none could understand. After a long time, someone said, "Why does he have life?" The temple deity replied, "The village person Cui." The God of Zitong remained silent.

Fu Zi wished to know about his future prospects and went forward to bow, saying, "Lord, you oversee human affairs of merit and fame. My family has held official positions for three generations, all as provincial governors or prefectural magistrates. I do not know how far my future prospects will extend." The Lord replied, "Your family's wealth and status were all accumulated by your great-grandfather alone. Your great-grandfather and the three generations below him were all in charge of important affairs. But with their newfound wealth and status, they all became arrogant, lustful, greedy, and violent, so they no longer became prominent. Now, you will only be able to hold one position." Fu Zi asked, "What about my two sons?" The Lord said, "The eldest son can become a prefectural governor, and the younger one can become a canal commissioner, both within forty years." Fu Zi was pleased. A temple deity asked, "What brings you here?" Fu Zi explained the reason, and the deity said, "Your father may be chasing you, but your mother is thinking of you. You should hurry back, so as not to worry your mother." Fu Zi left the temple and, looking around, saw nothing. He returned home, and his mother was crying and searching with a candle. Three years later, Fu Zi died, realizing that the god's words about one position referred to three years. During the Yuan dynasty when the south was pacified, the eldest son met with soldiers and died after being stabbed three times, because three strokes make the character for prefecture. The younger son drowned in the Cao River, forming the character for canal commissioner.

Emperor Qinzong arrived at Yuanchang Prefecture and stayed overnight in a temple outside the city. There were no Buddha statues in the hall, only two stone carvings of Hu women present. Ghost fires flickered, dispersing and reuniting. Suddenly, someone appeared carrying wine and food, saying, "This temple has the most powerful deities." The night before, they reported a dream saying, "Tomorrow night, there will be Emperor Tianluo, dressed in a green robe, coming from the south to stay here. That is why he came here to serve." After the emperor drank, someone then led him into a hillside area, where there were three thatched cottages. Entering the door, they heard the sound of bows, as if from more than thirty people. Everyone was surprised. Looking at the deity, it was also a stone carving of a woman, looking like a general, holding an iron sword. Her attendants were all women. When the emperor left, he again heard the sound of bows as before. When asked, they said it was the temple of the Heavenly Emperor of the Khitan. The emperor then realized that his previous life was Emperor Tianluo.

During the Northern Expedition, the two Song emperors arrived at a temple where there were two stone carvings of Vajra, standing with hands clasped. Upon entering the temple, they saw a foreign monk coming and going, and the statues of the deities were tall and imposing, their heads touching the rafters. There were no other offerings, only a stone bowl and an incense burner. The monk bowed and invited them to sit, asking, "Where do you come from?" The emperor replied that he came from the south. The monk called a boy to prepare tea, which tasted very fragrant and delicious. When they wanted more tea, the monk and the boy rushed to the back of the hall and left. After some time, they returned, but the room was empty and desolate, with only a small room in the bamboo grove. Inside the room, there was a carved statue of a foreign monk and two child attendants, who looked exactly like those who served tea. Li Shen, a man from Longxi, was the son of someone who had once received favors from Zhu Ci. When the rebels were defeated and punished, Li Shen escaped and was spared. Later, he encountered amnesty and gave away all his property, servants, and children to Huilin Temple in Luoyang and became a monk. He spent his days reading and playing the qin, just to pass the time. Today, Cui Qun, a prominent official from Qinghe, who passed the imperial examinations, treated him with the respect due to a disciple. Cui Qun was well-known for his associations, and he often had close friendships with scholars. In the autumn of the 13th year of Emperor Xianzong's reign, Li Zuo told Li Shen, "I have some business to attend to in Song, but the return date is uncertain. Can you take me to Song by boat?" Li Shen, feeling a deep longing, boarded the boat. They initially agreed to travel one stage, but when they reached the end of the stage, Li Shen said, "Your feelings are not limited to this." They continued their journey, and Li Shen, moved by emotion, could not bring himself to say goodbye, and they went directly to Suizhou.

At dusk, Zhe Zhe dismissed the boatman and departed, then sat with Shen on the Bian River embankment under the moonlight. He spoke slowly: "Your remembrance has been sincere for a long time. Do you know who I am?" Shen replied, "You are a learned and eloquent scholar!" Zhe Zhe said, "No, I am a spirit official. Recently, I was the governor of Luo Prefecture, which is why I spent much time in Luoyang. I handle public affairs discreetly, which is why I cannot act during the day, and thus I can now meet with you. Now, I am transitioning from the spirit world to the human realm, and I have been pregnant in my mother's womb for five years. That is why I have been inviting you step by step; I have a purpose in mind." Shen asked, "What is it?" Zhe Zhe replied, "My body is unique and difficult to match. I am concerned that if I leave this form, I will be entirely consumed by my past deeds. I ask you to help me awaken. However, I have been pregnant in my mother's womb for five years, and my family considers this an omen. They have exhausted all resources to perform rituals and prayers, but I have not yet been born, and the gods have not intervened. You should go to my family and write the character for 'birth' in red ink, then have them swallow it. I will be born, and I will certainly present you with silk and paper; you will also gain a daughter. After three years, you should come back to see me and say: 'The owner's granddaughter has not given birth for a long time. I swallowed the red character, and she gave birth to a son who is exceptionally wise. He is now three years old. I thought about it and divined it, so I have come back.' You can take the child and hold it while sleeping. Wait until the caretaker closes the door, then hold it in a quiet place and call out, 'Li Zhe, do you remember me?' The child will cry, and when it cries, hold it. I will certainly awaken completely."

The elder brother should speak to Zuo about the places in Luoyang where he lives and entertains guests. Zuo will definitely have a profound realization. After this realization, his life's work will be completely successful, and he will definitely return to his original state or path. Zuo will achieve great prosperity and honor later, but the elder brother will not have the same opportunity to remain at ease. The elder brother has a very good reputation and will soon receive a high-ranking position in the government. He should be cautious and not accept the position, as doing so might not be beneficial for his longevity. There is a village called Hu Village, located 20 miles north, with a gate in front of it, which is Zuo's personal residence. After finishing his words, he wept and bowed before departing. By dawn, Shen, carrying a walking stick, visited the place. Indeed, there was a Hu family there, and he knocked on the door and pleaded earnestly. The elderly man in charge, over eighty years old, leaned on a walking stick and invited him in. Once seated, the old man seemed worried, and Shen asked him about it. The old man said, "My new wife has been pregnant for five years now, and despite all our efforts, there are no signs of labor." Shen replied, "I have been attentive in the Daoist arts and am quite skilled in incantations. I can discern the reason for her lack of delivery." He immediately ordered the servants to bring the pregnant woman. Shen examined her arm and said, "It is a boy, very bright and wise, with extraordinary talent, hence he is not bound by the usual time of birth!" Then he ordered them to quickly prepare the bedding and furniture in the delivery room. Shen took a pen as if to light incense, wrote the character for "birth" in red, and had her swallow it. As soon as she did, a boy was born.

The old man was extremely pleased and presented thirty bolts of silk. Shen then accepted it and said, "This child is not ordinary; at three years old, he will come again, and I will help you divinate his fate." After speaking, he left. When the time came, he went again and said, "The child born before is now three years old. I wish to keep him for one night to divinate his fate." The caretaker was pleased and agreed. At night, Shen waited for people to be quiet, carried the child to a distant place, and called out, "Li Zuo, do you recognize me now?" The child cried in fear, and Shen held him, saying, "Li Zuo, why don't you remember seeing me?" He held the child again, and the child cried even more. Shen held the child and asked him several times, and the child suddenly said, "Could the Sixteenth Brother really come here?" Shen then spoke with the child about matters in Luoyang, and they both laughed greatly, as if they had known each other all their lives. Shen said, "Li Zuo has now understood everything," and carried him back to his dwelling. The next morning, Shen told the caretaker, "This child will have a high position; he is a noble person for the family. He should be kept and protected." Hu was pleased and gave another fifty bolts of silk. They then parted ways, and Shen remembered Xing Su's words, which suggested that the three talents and five stars concealed his destiny. Because Shen, who received his salary, was punished, and the one who did not receive it was spared, it was his fate. This is enough to warn those who greedily seek positions and do not know their fate.

In the Jingzhao region, Wei Andao set out early one morning and arrived at Cihuili. He saw a retinue resembling imperial guards, with a delicate flying umbrella adorned like a jade maiden. A supervisor riding behind pointed to the west gate of the neighborhood and said, "Go from here, and you will know." Following his instructions, Wei Andao knocked on the door, and a red-clothed official emerged and said, "The Lady of the Earth has been waiting for you!" He was led to a large city, where the western part was near the Yellow River and Fen River. To the north of the city was a grand gate, with guards standing in rows in the hall. He barely heard the sound of jade pendants, and the supervisor said, "The lady and you are destined to be a couple!" They were led inside and sat opposite each other. Soon after, food was served, and musicians played a double phoenix melody. Then, the attendants led Wei Andao into the tent, and they performed the traditional ceremony of drinking from a shared wine cup to signify marriage. The lady was still a virgin. The next day, the lady wished to meet her in-laws. Wei Andao's parents were surprised when they saw her. A servant sent Wei Andao to speak on behalf of them, asking her to leave. The lady said, "If the in-laws have given orders, how dare we not obey?" The next day, the lady wore official robes and resided in the grand hall. She summoned all the kings of the world to come, and the last person to arrive was identified as the Great Luo Heavenly Maiden. Upon closer inspection, she was the Empress. The lady spoke to the Empress, asking for five million coins and a fifth-rank official position for Wei Andao, and to return home.

贾知征在洞庭湖上暂住，于是吟诵了一首怀古诗：“极目远眺烟波中的九嶷山，吟诗的灵魂因见傍晚的鸿雁飞去而忧愁。两位妃子是否有怨恨你知道吗？为何已经过了十天还未归来。”来到岳阳，又作诗道：“湖面平静，天上的云彩仿佛被草丛遮蔽，偶然停泊在巴陵的旧水边。可惜仙女们没有用心，张颀并不是有才华的人。”不久，看到一艘莲花形的船上有几位女子，她们弹奏着瑟缓缓下船，贾知征目送她们离去。船抵达西岸，这里是曾城夫人的京兆君府邸，贾知征快步进入厅堂，看到已经准备好了筵席，有三位女子，一位自称曾城夫人，一位自称湘君夫人，一位自称湘夫人，酒过三巡后，她们各自请求吟诗，贾知征说道：“偶然划着小船在茫茫湖面上漂泊，没想到有幸来到仙人的故乡。玉堂长久以来照耀着星辰聚集，雪白的扇子打开，日月的光芒长久不息。岂止是忍受怜悯成为上宾，还能在宴会上欢笑。”

At dawn, he feared they would part and fly away, and the person on the garment smelled a strange fragrance. "The Lord of Xiang said: 'Looking south toward Cangwu, my jade-like face is filled with sorrow; the colors of the Nine疑Mountains are layered upon each other. One must know that the place of evening rain and morning clouds is not limited to the Twelve Peaks of the Yangtze River.' The Lady of Xiang said: 'At night, singing lotus songs and entering Dongting Lake, the lotus pickers wear green algae. Singing a long song and returning empty-handed, do not let the lotus flowers belong to anyone else.' The Lord of Jingzhao said: 'A messenger bird lands on a reed bank, and I follow the divine chariot back to the city of the gods. Grieving the distant farewell to Zhang Sheng, I gain a reputation for being unfaithful in the human world.' When the poem was finished, the two ladies of Xiang departed, and the Lord of Jingzhao invited him to stay overnight. The next day, he gave him fifty pills of the longevity medicine wrapped in a silk handkerchief. After receiving them, he recited a poem in gratitude: 'The medicine is the longevity elixir from the city of the gods, and the handkerchief is the autumn cloud silk of the weaving girl. With great care, it is given to a traveler, to express the abundance of love and longing.' He then bowed and departed, walking a hundred steps from the shore, and when he looked back, the lady's house had disappeared.

Wei Ziqing, having been recommended as a filial and upright official, arrived at the Huayin Temple. After drinking to his heart's content, he wandered into the courtyard of the three daughters and saw one of them as exceptionally beautiful. He said, "When I return after passing the imperial examination, I will marry the third daughter as my wife." That spring, he passed the examination and, on his way back to Weibei, saw a man in yellow robes who said, "The Great King has sent a command!" Wei Ziqing was startled, and soon after, he saw a grand procession of carriages and horses, accompanied by a lady adorned with a golden seal and a purple sash. After they had exchanged pleasantries, they chose a date for the wedding ceremony. Twenty days later, Wei said, "You may return now." His wife said, "I am a spirit, and I am not a match for you. When you reach Songzhou, the governor will arrange for his daughter to marry you. Do not reveal my secret, for if it is exposed, it will be detrimental to both of us!" Wei Ziqing reached Songzhou, and the governor indeed proposed marriage, and Wei accepted. The spirit wife often visited him and said, "You have recently gained a beautiful wife, but do not forget your past promises." Later, the governor's daughter fell ill, and despite treatment, she did not recover. A Taoist priest, skilled in magical rituals, said that Wei had an evil aura, which had caused the illness in the governor's daughter. Using a talisman, the priest summoned Wei and interrogated him, and Wei recounted the entire story. The Taoist priest then flew a black talisman to summon the spirit wife and said, "Although you are not guilty, as a spirit, you dare not interfere with the affairs of the living." He then punished her with five strokes of a cane. A month later, the governor's daughter died, and Wei suddenly saw the spirit wife and said, "I warned you not to reveal my secret, fearing that misfortune would befall me, and now it has come to pass!" The spirit wife shouted at her attendants, "Do not give the hand of death, what are you waiting for!" The attendants dragged Wei and beat him, and that night, he died.

Miscellaneous Records of Strange Events, Volume Three Liquan Magistrate Cui Fen's elder brother lived in Chongxian Lane, Chang'an. On a summer night, he cooled off in the spacious courtyard. The moonlight was at its brightest, and the wind passed, and he sensed a strange fragrance. After a while, he heard the earth on the southern wall trembling and making a rustling sound. Cui Fen thought it was a snake or a mouse. Suddenly, he saw a Taoist priest who loudly said, "What a beautiful moon!" Cui Fen was frightened and hurriedly ran away. The Taoist priest walked slowly in the courtyard, about forty years old, with a dignified and ancient appearance. After a long time, more than ten courtesans pushed open the main gate and entered, wearing silk and jade, adorned with green ornaments, and exceedingly beautiful and captivating. Their attendants prepared fragrant tea, and they sat in a row under the moon. Cui Fen suspected they were enchanting and threw a pillow at the door to startle them. The Taoist priest glanced angrily and said, "I chose this place because it was relatively quiet and also because of the beautiful moon. I had no intention of staying, how dare you act so rudely?" He then raised his voice and asked, "Is there any boundary here?" Suddenly, two people appeared, only three feet tall, with large heads and prominent ears, and they bowed before him.

The Taoist gestured towards Cui's place and said, "Does this person have any relatives recorded in the underworld's registry? Can you bring them here!" Two people rushed out. After a short while, Cui saw his parents and brother arrive, accompanied by dozens of guards who dragged and beat them. The Taoist scolded, "I am here; how dare you allow your children to be so disrespectful?" Cui's parents explained, "The living and the dead are separated; we cannot receive instruction or reprimand." The Taoist scolded them and sent them away. He then turned to the two ghosts and said, "Catch this foolish person!" The two ghosts leaped to the door and threw a red object like a bullet from afar into Cui's mouth. It was a fine red rope, which dragged him out into the courtyard, where they insulted and humiliated him. Cui was so shocked that he lost his voice and could not defend himself. His servants and concubines all cried out in grief. One of Cui's concubines knelt and bowed in a circle, saying, "He is just an ordinary person. He was surprised at the unexpected arrival of the divine official and had no fault." The Taoist's anger subsided, and he waved his sleeves and left through the main gate. Cui fell ill as if poisoned and took five or six days to recover. He then invited a wine master for a ritual offering and thanksgiving, and there was no further trouble. Initially, Cui saw his deceased brother through a gap in the paper. His brother's face mask had a damaged lower lip, which the servants and attendants found surprising. One maid cried, "When the young master was about to be buried, they forgot to open the mouth of the face mask. In their haste, they accidentally injured his lower lip. No one else saw it, but in the underworld for twenty years, he still bears this suffering!"

Once upon a time, there was a scholar who passed the imperial examination and attended a public banquet. During the meal, a courtesan of unparalleled beauty presented a cup with solemnity, never touching it herself, which raised suspicions among the guests. One guest, slightly tipsy, playfully asked, "Are you six-fingered?" When he forcibly pulled her, she fell over, revealing that she was actually a mummy. Shortly after, the scholar was assigned a post and died immediately. Yue Kē, the grandson of the famous general Yue Fei, served as the governor of Jiaxing. One night, the drum at the watchtower failed to sound at the first watch, prompting the governor to interrogate the night watchman. The watchman reported that every night at the first watch, five people would come to the tower to drink, all using gold and silver vessels and arranged with delicacies. They claimed to be relatives of the vice-premier and thus dared not strike the drum. The governor instructed the watchman to secretly inform him if they came again that night. That night, the governor sat in the fragrant hall and ordered two officials to bring the seal of the prefecture forward. He selected twenty elite soldiers, each armed with weapons, to wait below the hall. At midnight, the watchman indeed reported that the five people were drinking at the watchtower. The governor, accompanied by the officials and the seal, approached and said, "The vice-premier of Jiaxing, Yue Kē, requests your presence!" The five people scattered in fright. The governor sat in the center, took a look at the vessels, and found them to be genuine gold and silver. He ordered them to be used by the public treasury, and the evil spirits ceased.

In Yongjia, the Xiang family's house was occupied by evil spirits. There was a creature that appeared human but had wild hair, coming and going in the house, and it would call itself "Taigong." The family had grown accustomed to this and did not find it strange. Whenever they had a request, they would just call Taigong in the kitchen, and the creature would appear. One day, Xiang's wife was pregnant and wanted to eat steamed buns. She called Taigong, and after the second watch, the creature arrived with a steamer of steamed buns, the steam still warm. Several days later, people heard that a family at the Seven Chi Crossing had set up a vegetarian meal and lost a steamer of steamed buns. Later, Xiang's wife gave birth to a child shaped like a winter melon, without eyebrows or eyes, but with a mouth capable of suckling. She was about to drown the child when suddenly she heard Taigong speaking from the air, saying, "Do not drown the child. Temporarily feed it, and there will be a way to thank you." After two months, Xiang's wife was holding the child in bed when Taigong placed two gold tablets on the bed and took the child away. After that, the strange occurrences ceased.

#### First Part Once upon a time, there were several scholar friends from the Imperial Academy in Beijing who were taking a moonlit walk at night. They saw a young servant holding a red silk lantern in front, followed by a woman who was slowly trailing behind. The scholars were suspicious of the strange sight of a woman walking alone at night and decided to follow and observe her. They reached the left side of Zhong'an Bridge and knocked on the door of the internal physician Zhang Yufang to request medicine. Zhang opened the door and saw her, then immediately closed the door and refused to admit her. Next, they knocked on the door of Li Tianding's clinic. Li came out and invited her for a pulse diagnosis, but the scholars waited for a long time without him coming out. They marked the doors of the two doctors and returned home. The next day, they visited Zhang Yufang and said, "Walking alone at night, she must not be a virtuous woman, which is why you refused her." Next, they passed by Li's clinic and heard mourning and crying inside. When they asked about it, they were told, "Last night, a woman knocked on our door to request medicine. After she left, she suffered a stroke and died." It was then that they realized the ghost had transformed into a woman, knocking on the door to seek medical help. Is it not because Li was moved by her beauty and thus caused this? #### Second Part Young Master Ma Shao, when he was young, was reading by the light of a candle near the window. Suddenly, a large hand like a fan appeared and passed through the window lattice. The next night, it came again. Master Ma dipped his brush in realgar water and wrote a flower seal on the window, calling out from outside, "Quickly wash it away for me, otherwise, disaster will befall you!" He ignored the call and went to sleep. After a while, the ghost became very angry and demanded that the seal be washed away more urgently, but Master Ma paid no attention. As dawn approached, the ghost wailed and could not withdraw its hand, saying, "You will become greatly honored, just to test you, how can you let me reach such a dire state! Have you not heard of Wen Qiao's use of the rhinoceros horn to see ghosts?" Master Ma greatly understood and washed away the flower seal with water. The hand finally withdrew, and he saw nothing.

Xu Xi served as the magistrate of Sheyang and was young but skilled in medical formulas, famous throughout the land. Once, he heard a ghost moaning at night, its voice very sad and painful. Xu asked, "You are a ghost, what do you need?" Soon after, he heard a reply: "My name is Hush, my home is in Dongchang, and I died from a waist pain. Although I am a ghost, the pain is unbearable. I have heard that you are skilled in moxibustion, I wish you could help me." Xu said, "You are a ghost and have no form, where should I treat you?" The ghost replied, "Just tie straw into a person shape and needle the holes." Xu followed the instructions and needled four places on the waist and three on the shoulders and back, then set up a sacrifice and buried the straw figure. The next day, a person came to thank him, saying, "Thanks to your medical treatment, I was able to set up a sacrifice again, my pain is gone, and I am no longer hungry. Your kindness is deeply appreciated!" Suddenly, the person disappeared. Tang Han served as the governor of Nanzuan and often told people about an incident during the Zhongde era. There was a person named Cui who was selected as the postal inspector of Tongzhou. A man in green robes, whose name was unknown, bowed to him and they rode together. Xu asked what his position was, and the man in green robes said, "I have just been appointed as the postal inspector of Tongzhou." Cui said, "I have just been appointed to this position, could it be a mistake?" The man in green robes smiled without answering and they continued on their way, all saying they were going to take up their posts. About dozens of miles from Tongzhou, officials bowed to greet them on a side road. The man in green robes told Cui, "You are the recorder of the Yangdao, and I am the recorder of the Yin dao. We part here, shouldn't we bid farewell?" Cui was surprised and immediately rode together into the side road. They then arrived at a city, with streets and government offices, all very grand.

The green-robed man arrived at the hall, sat with Cui Sheng, and received an audience. After summoning officials, clerks, monks, and Taoists, he then dealt with lawsuits, prisoners, and legal matters. Cui Sheng was greatly surprised and asked the green-robed man, "How did my humble wife come to be here?" The green-robed man immediately hid behind the desk and allowed Cui Sheng to meet his wife. His wife said, "I was summoned here and have been here for several days. You should plead for me with the recorder!" Cui Sheng then begged the green-robed man, who ordered the clerks to quickly release Cui Sheng's wife. Cui Sheng asked his wife what crime she had committed to be there. The green-robed man replied, "Your family is in Tongzhou, and the deceased are all being examined. It seems you are in charge of the path of the living." Cui Sheng stayed for half a day before asking to return. The green-robed man ordered the clerks to pay their respects and bid farewell, saying, "Though there is a distinction between the living and the dead, we are all from Tongzhou. Shouldn't we pay respects to the supervisor before parting?" The green-robed man also saw him off with a banquet in the suburbs, being very hospitable. He waved his sleeve and sent him off at the crossroads. When Cui Sheng returned to Tongzhou, he asked his wife about her condition. She had been ill for seven or eight days, completely unconscious, and her spirit was not in control. Now, she had just regained consciousness. Cui Sheng remembered the day he was released. His wife did not remember the path of the dead; when she saw Cui Sheng, she realized it, like a dream, and did not clearly recall it.

The Yellow Gate Attendant Lu Gong Huan served as an official in Mingzhou. In a remote and desolate area of Xiangshan County, a group of tomb robbers discovered an ancient tomb. They reported that they first saw a carriage track with patterned bricks, which led them to uncover the tomb. The robbers gathered ten accomplices, submitted a petition to the county, and requested permission to stay by the roadside. The county magistrate approved their request, and they planted hemp to conceal their activities, ensuring no outsiders could see what they were doing. They then exerted all their efforts to excavate the tomb. Entering the entrance passage, they gradually reached the main chamber, where they found three stone doors sealed with iron. The robbers first recited incantations and fasted to purify themselves. The next day, the two doors opened, revealing hundreds of bronze figures and horses in each door, holding spears and halberds, all exquisitely crafted.

The thief again fasted for three days. One door of the middle gate opened, and a man in yellow robes emerged, delivering a message: "A messenger from Han General Liu (whose name is forgotten) has come to inquire. Someone had great merit in military campaigns and was granted a protected burial, as well as the casting of bronze figures of humans and horses to represent the ceremonial guards during his lifetime. You have come here with the intention of obtaining wealth, but the rooms behind the two doors actually do not contain other valuables. Official burials do not bury treasures; why trouble us with your magical incantations? If you do not stop, both parties will suffer!" After speaking, he returned inside, and the door closed as before. The thief continued to recite the incantation for several days. The two doors suddenly opened, and a great flood washed away everything, drowning all the thieves. One thief managed to escape, bound himself, and went to the authorities, explaining everything in detail. The Yellow Gate Commander inspected the site and found that inside the middle door, there was a stone bed, on which the remains were lying, and the water had washed half of the remains down. They then sealed the two rooms and blocked the tunnel.

In the third year of the Changqing era, Qi Tui, the governor of Xiaozhou, had a daughter who was married to Wei Hui, a military officer in Huzhou. As Wei Hui was about to be summoned for a new posting, he sent his wife back to Poyang, her hometown, and then traveled to the capital. On the night of the eleventh month, when his wife was about to give birth, Lady Qi suddenly saw a man over a zhang tall, clad in golden armor and holding a halberd, who angrily said, "I am General Chen from the Liang Dynasty, and I have long lived in this house. Who are you to dare to defile this place?" He raised his halberd to kill her, and Lady Qi cried out, pleading, "My eyes are limited, and I did not know that a general lived here. Since I have been here, please allow me to move away." The general replied, "If you do not move, you will die." The people around her heard Lady Qi's cries and rushed to her side. She was drenched in sweat and in a state of shock. They asked her what she had seen, and she slowly described it. By morning, the female servant informed the governor, asking to be moved to another room. The governor, who adhered to the belief that there were no ghosts, did not listen.
