At night, he slept in front of her, using himself as a support. In the hall, they added people and lit more candles to comfort her. At midnight, he heard Qi's startled and painful cry. He opened the door and entered to look, and found that she had died with a head injury. The magistrate's grief and anger reached its peak, far more intense than usual. He thought to cut himself with a knife, but found it insufficient to atone for his daughter. He then buried her in a separate room and sent a swift messenger to inform Wei Hui. Wei, having made a minor mistake in his records, was demoted by the Celestial Official. He sought reinstatement through unusual means and did not encounter the bad news of Qi's death. A hundred miles away from Raozhou, he suddenly saw a woman. Her appearance and gait were very similar to Qi's. He called his servant and pointed at her, saying, "Have you seen that person? How does she resemble my wife!" The servant replied, "My lady is the beloved daughter of the governor, why would she do this? It's just that people can look alike!" Wei scrutinized her and found it even more so. He leaped onto his horse and approached, and the woman then entered the door, slightly closing it. Thinking it might be someone else, he did not dismount and passed by.

Turning back to look, Qi came out from the door and called out, "Wei, how could you not look at me!" Wei quickly dismounted from his horse and looked. It was his wife. He was startled and asked her why, and she fully recounted the matter of General Chen. She then wept and said, "I am indeed foolish and unrefined, fortunately serving as your attendant, but I have never offended a gentleman. I was just about to devote myself completely to the home, to grow old with you, but I was unjustly killed by a mad ghost. Checking my own life records, I should have been eighteen years old. Now there is a way to save myself, can you show me mercy?" The depth of her sorrow and anger was such that words could not fully express it. Wei said, "The bond between husband and wife is equal in meaning, like birds and fish that always stay together. There is no way to be alone in this body, where else could I go? If there is a path, even fire and boiling water can be entered. But life and death are different paths, the dark and obscure are hard to know. How can I show my sincerity? I wish to hear your plan." She said, "A few miles east of this village, there is a thatched cottage. Mr. Tian, who leads village children in teaching, is there. This person is strange, and cannot be spoken of hastily. Can you dismount and walk, approach the door and pay respects, as if paying respect to a superior official, crying while pleading for justice. He will definitely be very angry, including scolding, humiliating, beating, dragging, and spitting. Only then will he feel pity, and then I will definitely return."

The appearance of the gentleman, indeed, does not match; mysterious and obscure matters, please do not neglect. "Thus they traveled together, Wei led the horse and gave it to him. Qi Shi smiled and said, 'Now my body is no longer as it was. Although you ride a horse, it is still difficult to catch up. The matter is very urgent, please do not refuse.' Wei spurred his horse to follow, but often could not keep up. After traveling several miles, they saw a thatched cottage on the northern side of the road. Qi Shi pointed and said, 'This is where the gentleman resides. If your heart is truly determined to save me, do not retreat despite any hardship. If he has been insulted, I will surely return. Do not let anger cloud your face, for it may lead to a permanent separation. Encourage yourself, and from here, I bid you farewell!' She waved her tears and departed. Within a few steps, she suddenly disappeared. Wei collected his tears and went to the thatched cottage. Before reaching it by several hundred steps, he dismounted, dressed in formal attire, and had his servant hold the introduction scroll ahead. When he arrived at the front of the hall, the students said, 'The gentleman has changed his meal and has not yet returned.' Wei held his hand tablet and waited. After a long time, a person came wearing a tattered hat and dragging wooden sandals, with an extremely ugly appearance. When asked by his disciples, they said it was the gentleman. Wei ordered his servant to present the introduction scroll, and he hurriedly bowed and greeted. The gentleman returned the bow and said, 'I am an old village man, seeking food from young herders. What is a gentleman doing here, it is very surprising.' Wei bowed and pleaded, 'My wife Qi Shi, who did not live to half her life, was wrongfully killed by General Chen of the Liang Dynasty. I humbly beg to be allowed to return home, to finish my remaining years!' Because of this, he knocked the ground and wept while bowing."

The gentleman said, "I am just a simple, ignorant old man from the countryside, and my disciples are in dispute; I cannot even resolve their differences, let alone matters of the afterlife. Are you not acting foolishly? Go away immediately, and do not indulge in absurd talk!" Without hesitation, he entered. Wei knelt before the bed and said, "I truly recount a deep injustice; may you show mercy and forgive me!" The gentleman looked at his disciples and said, "This person is suffering from a mental illness, has come here to cause trouble, and should be pushed out. Let him in and out again, and you should spit on him!" A dozen or so village children came and spat on his face, and it was clear how filthy it was. Wei dared not wipe the spittle and knelt again, speaking earnestly and sincerely. The gentleman said, "I have heard that people with mental illnesses do not feel pain when beaten, so let the students beat him for me. He cannot withstand it, only his face will be bruised!" The village children came again and beat him, causing him unbearable pain. Wei held a tablet and stood respectfully, enduring the blows. After the beating, he knelt again and begged in a sorrowful manner. The gentleman then ordered his disciples to push him down, grab his feet, and drag him out, repeating this three times. The gentleman said to his disciples, "This person truly knows that I have a skill, which is why he came to visit. You may go now, I will save him."

The children had all dispersed, and someone said to Wei, "Indeed, you are a man of great resolve. For the sake of your wife's wrongs, you willingly endure humiliation. Moved by your sincerity, I will help to investigate." Therefore, he ordered Wei to enter the room. Inside the room, a clean mat was laid out, and on the mat, there was a table with an incense burner placed on it. In front of the incense burner, another mat was laid out. They sat down, and Wei saw a man in a yellow robe leading them northward. They traveled hundreds of miles, entering a walled city where the streets and alleys were noisy, just like a major city. There was another small city to the north, with towering towers and grand halls, as if it were a royal residence. Guards holding weapons stood and sat, each numbering several hundred. When they reached the gate, the gatekeeper announced, "The former magistrate of Huzhou, Wei." They proceeded through and entered, reaching a grand hall with nine bays facing north. One bay in the hall had the curtains drawn, and a bed and table were set up. A man in purple robes sat facing south. Wei entered and bowed to the seated man. He stood up and looked, and it was Master Tian. Wei again recounted his grievances. The attendants nearby in the west passed on the petition, and Wei hurriedly went into the western corridor. Someone handed him a brush and inkstone, and he wrote a petition. Wei asked who was in charge, and they said it was King Wang. The official collected the petition and presented it to the hall. King Wang decreed, "Summon General Chen!" and had the petition reviewed. The decree was issued, within moments, and announced, "Bring General Chen here!" Dressed in armor and wielding halberds as described by Qi, General Chen arrived.

The King asked, "Why did you unjustly kill an ordinary person?" The General replied, "I have lived in this room for hundreds of years, and Qi has defiled it. I was repeatedly pardoned but remained unrepentant, and in anger, I killed him. My crime deserves a thousand deaths!" The King judged, "Light and darkness take different paths, principles do not interfere. A long-dead spirit illegally occupies someone's room, does not reflect on itself, and continues to kill the innocent. It can be sentenced to one hundred strokes and exiled to the south of the East Sea!" The clerk reviewed the record and said, "Qi's fortune and fate actually have twenty-eight years." The King ordered Qi to be called, asking if his earthly fortune was not yet exhausted, and if it was right for him to return. He was to be released back to the world, and he asked if Qi wished to return. Qi said, "I truly wish to return." The King judged, "Hand it over to the case and order a return." The clerk reported, "Qi's house is destroyed, and there is nowhere for him to return to." The King said, "Send someone to repair it." The clerk said, "Everything is destroyed, and repairs cannot be made." The King said, "He must be released back to the world!" They went out to discuss the matter, and soon returned, saying, "There is only one option: to release the living soul, there is no other plan." The King said, "What is the difference between a soul and a living person?" The clerk replied, "The only difference is that on the day when the year is up and death is due, the person is gravely ill but has no body." The King summoned Wei and said, "A living soul only differs in this way." Wei bowed and requested, and the King ordered Qi to return together. They each bowed and left, and the man in yellow robes led them south again. They immediately left the city, as if walking through cliffs and valleys, and fell down with a stumble. Opening his eyes, he immediately knelt in front of the desk, and the teacher also sat at the desk.

The master said, "This matter is extremely secret, and without your sincerity, it cannot be obtained. However, your noble wife has not yet been buried, still lying in the old house. It is advisable to send a swift letter to arrange her burial, and once she is buried, there will be no more suffering. Be careful not to speak of this to the prefecture, for if it is even slightly revealed to others, it will be detrimental to the magistrate. Your noble residence is just at the door, so you can go together." Wei bowed and thanked him before leaving. By then, his wife was already waiting in front of the horse, and at that moment, she was a living person, no longer light and agile. Wei threw her clothes and saddlebag aside, letting his wife ride it, and he crossed to follow her. He also sent a swift letter to the prefecture, requesting the burial of her coffin. The magistrate first heard that Wei was coming and set up a hall, hanging white curtains to await him. Upon receiving the letter, he was shocked and did not believe it, but he forced the burial and ordered his wife and children to meet Wei with a sedan chair. Seeing Wei, the magistrate became even more troubled and asked him in many ways, but Wei did not reveal the truth. That night, the magistrate got Wei drunk with wine and pressed him to ask. Unconsciously, Wei revealed everything. The magistrate heard and became angry, and soon after, he fell ill, dying after several months. Wei secretly sent someone to spy on Mr. Tian, but he also did not know the reason. Qi's diet, birth, and reproduction were no different from usual, but she was carried in a sedan chair, and she did not feel like a person. I have heard of this for a long time, but I have not fully believed it. In the autumn of Taihe Year 2, the magistrate of Fuping, Song Jiandun, spoke of strange events at a gathering. There was a guest, Zhang Qi, who was a military advisor to the Duke of Fufeng and Wei's maternal cousin. He fully described this matter, with no difference from the old news, and said, "Qi's sister-in-law is still alive. After returning, I have already paid my respects to her. Her spirit and appearance are better than before. The management of officials in the underworld is indeed mysterious. Is it not a false statement?"

In the first year of the Changqing era, when Lord Tian Hongzheng lost his position in Zhenyang, Wang Tai, an imperial scholar, was a guest there. Hearing that war had broken out, he fled south of the city. At that time, the armies were clashing in the fields, so he hid during the day and traveled at night, entering Xindu for five or six li. Suddenly, there was a yellow dog that followed him. Soon, the dog turned to Wang Tai and said, "This road is extremely dangerous. Why are you traveling at night?" Wang Tai remained silent for a long time and said, "It's the trouble in Zhenyang." The dog replied, "However, if I meet with Speedy Fly, it would be your good fortune too. Allow Speedy Fly to be your servant, and then you will have no worries." Wang Tai privately told his wife, "Those who act recklessly in the open will be blamed by others; those who act recklessly in the hidden or dark places will be punished by ghosts. But now my actions are not reckless. So what can be done to me? Even the gods and spirits do not fear me. How much less so an evil dog? Therefore, I can surely control it with righteousness." So he agreed. Suddenly, the dog transformed into a person, bowing and saying, "I am fortunate to serve. However, Speedy Fly is slow in movement. Please allow me to temporarily become a donkey. Let me borrow Speedy Fly to ride on it, and then I can follow along." Wang Tai was surprised and did not respond. So he drove his servant down the road. Before taking a few steps, he did not realize that he had become a donkey. The dog then rode on him. Wang Tai was very afraid, but he had no way to control it. He could only rely on his righteous heart.

They all walked ten li, and on the left side of the road, there was something with a body length of several feet and a head and face twice as long as its body, red-eyed and bearded. Raising his eyebrows and smiling, he said, "How can you serve people, Quick Fly?" The dog replied, "I have already pledged myself to a person." The creature then said, "Sir, please do not be afraid." The big-headed one hit him in the face and ran away. Another few li, they encountered a creature with a large face and many eyes, with flashing red light, who called out, "How can you serve people, Quick Fly?" The dog responded as before, and the multi-eyed one also fled. Quick Fly happily said, "These two creatures consider humans as their delicacy. When they get humans, they playfully throw them and fight over eating them, only after they are exhausted. Now that they have left, there is nothing to fear. Another three or five li, there was an elderly resident named Liu, whose family was not poor, and it was possible to take a short rest." Soon they arrived there, and it was a splendid mansion. The dog knocked on the door, and someone answered and came out, an elderly man over seventy years old, who walked very briskly. He opened the door and happily said, "How could Quick Fly bring such a distinguished guest?" The dog explained, "I traveled to Jizhou but did not meet anyone. I returned to the mountain pass and happened to serve Mr. Wang, who encountered difficulties in Zhenyang. He dared not travel during the day, so he arrived at night. Now I am extremely tired, and I wish to take a short rest." The elderly man said, "What is there that is not possible!" He then invited him in, housing him comfortably in the hall. Dishes, delicacies, fruits, and grains soon arrived, and there were also baskets of grass and grain for the horse. The transformed donkey also ate its fill.

Eating and sitting with Jiefei in advance, he said, "A tired traveler, who at night was given a fine meal, if there is more wine, the host's duty is fulfilled!" The old man said, "Without waiting to be asked, I had already ordered the dishes to be cleaned." Soon, a young servant brought out the wine, which was also very clean. The old man ordered Jiefei to pour the wine, and they drank together several rounds. Jiefei said, "Wine is not meant to be drunk silently. In general, in a household's joy, if a distinguished guest is not seen, who else will be seen?" The old man said, "The entertainers in the mountains are not enough to serve our enjoyment. How dare we be stingy?" He quickly summoned his favorite slave. After a short while, she was called in. She was a beautiful singer, about thirty years old, and she bowed to Tai and sat to his south. Her words and expressions were somewhat displeased. Tai asked her to sing, and she sang. When the old man asked, she always refused. The reason the favorite slave does not sing is because she feels sorry for not having a companion. The old man said, "There is a singer named Huayan nearby who is also good at singing, why not invite her?" The host quickly ordered her to be invited. After a short while, she was called in. She was a seventeen or eighteen-year-old girl, and her clothes were partly old and not very fresh. Huayan sat below the favorite slave. When it came to the old man's turn, he asked Huayan to sing, but when he asked the favorite slave, she refused. Her mood became even more displeased, as if she had something to complain about.

The protagonist visited the old man again, holding a cup and firmly inviting him to sing. The old man, feeling somewhat ashamed, laughed and said, "Usually, I ask for a song, and Chongnu has never refused. Now that there are a few guests, have you abandoned me? However, because of our old friendship, it should not be completely forgotten. Please sing one more song." Chongnu brushed his clothes and stood up, saying, "Liu Kun was killed by Duan Shidi, and Zhang Chongnu is now singing with this old fox!" All the lights went out, and the entire hall became dark. He cautiously looked outside and saw that it seemed bright. Then he roared out loudly and left, looking back at the hall, which turned out to be a large tomb. The horse was tied under a tall pine tree, and an old servant stood at the door. The moon was high in the sky. Tai asked his servant, "What did you do earlier?" The servant replied, "I dreamed of turning into a donkey, was ridden by someone, and ate grass with the horses." Tai then followed the path ahead and walked for more than ten miles. The sky grew light, and he met a farmer, asking, "What tomb is there nearby?" The farmer replied, "Within ten miles, there is the tomb of Zhang Chongnu, the mistress of Liu Kun, the Governor of Bingzhou during the Jin Dynasty." Tai then realized that this was where they had stayed the previous night. Another few miles down the road, he saw a rotten skull at the side of the road, with a hole nearby and grass growing inside it. Looking at it generally, it seemed to have four eyes, which is said to be the eye that summons flowers. Thinking of a person with a large head and many eyes, it was completely unknown. I have often viewed the world through the lens of Confucianism, believing that when people die, there are certainly ghosts. From the perspective of Buddhism, the principles of reincarnation are also clear. How can it be that this courtesan, forgotten for a thousand years, still sings in the depths of the underworld? Then it is true that beyond what can be seen or heard, sages and virtuous people also have things they do not speak of.

Ye Cheng was a dyer from Liangcheng in Zhongmou County, and his wife, Geng, had perceptive eyes. She often said, "Among all those who dwell, travel, farm, weave, trade, or sing and dance, half are humans and half are spirits. Spirits know they are not human, but humans do not recognize this." Their family had an ox with a red coat and horns, and the couple knew it was a remarkable animal. In the second year of the Yuanhe era, two spirits suddenly appeared, one resembling a state official and the other a local boundary spirit. They entered the pen and examined the ox, saying, "This ox is strong and beautiful, with impressive strength and a fine build. Our state has no such ox." The boundary spirit asked, "Why is this ox being pursued for a distant task?" The state official replied, "The princess of our state is marrying the second son of the Lord of the South Sea. There are 500 carriages in the procession, and the finest oxen from around the world have been selected. Ten oxen are needed from Henan, and the only suitable one in our state is this one. Should we report this?" They then departed. When Ye's wife saw the ox, she became anxious, gasping for breath and sweating profusely as if water was pouring down her face.

The old man was a dyer. He quickly took blue dye and applied it to the cow, just as he finished, there were military officials in purple robes riding dozens of horses, who laughed and entered. When they looked at the cow, it was different from what had been reported before. The military officials became very angry and seized the local official to punish him, accusing him, saying, "The noble lady is far away to marry, and the entire state chose a cow, and it was supposed to be this cow. How could it be false?" The old man replied, "I just confirmed with the local official, so I rushed to report. When I returned, the cow was gone, and it turned out the owner had hidden it. Please arrest the cow owner and question him; the cow is not far away." He then ordered the cow owner to be brought here. Several people climbed the steps and dragged the old man out. His family saw him as if he was possessed and could not respond. The young and old gathered around and cried, but the wife did not cry. She fetched water to wash the cow and removed the blue color, making it visible to the local official, who then took it away. The old man came back up the steps and, upon being called, stood up and said, "I was accused by the military officials of hiding the cow, but I was about to wash it when my wife did it herself, and thus I was released." When someone checked the cow, it was already dead. Yang Shu, who was governing Zhongmu, heard this story and summoned the people involved to ask about it, and everything matched up.

Tang nobleman Yuan Xuan, while traveling to Yu Luo Mountain, inquired about the remains of Xi Shi and left a poem on a stone, saying: "A thousand peaks on the mountain are beautiful, and the fine grass by the river is spring-like. Now I see the washing stone, but not the washer of the stone." He then turned around and saw a woman dressed in white and adorned with jade, who said to him, "I left the Wu Palace and the Yue State, wearing white clothes for a thousand years, no one recognizes me. At that time, my heart was as hard as gold and stone, but today I cannot be firm for you." Yuan Xuan understood her meaning and wrote another poem: "The beautiful lady has been gone for a thousand years, the stream and mountains have been lonely for a long time. Wild water floats with white mist, rocks bloom and wither on their own. Monkeys and cranes still have their old clear sounds, the wind and moon are noisy in the pavilions. Silently standing in the slanting sun, deep feelings enter the sky." Xi Shi said, "The poem is beautiful, but it does not fully express what I wish to convey." She then replied with a poem: "Tall flowers outside the rocks show affection at dawn, hidden birds cry without stopping in the rain. Red clouds fly over the western bank of the Yangtze River, from now on, people in the world will resent the wind and moon." As evening fell, they parted, and they agreed to meet the next day by the water's edge. The following day, Yuan Xuan went there, and Xi Shi was already waiting. From then on, he stayed for more than a month before returning. A man named Guo Su heard of this and also traveled to Yu Luo Mountain, leaving many poems in the springs and rocks, but found nothing and met no one. A nameless person mocked him, saying: "In the three springs, peach blossoms and plum flowers are silent, but the birds and sparrows of the past are noisy. If the neighbor to the east imitates Xi Shi, how does it compare to Guo Su learning from Yuan Xuan?" Those who heard this laughed.

Comprehensive Record of Strange Events, Volume 4 Fang Zizhang lived in Weitang Village, Xiu Prefecture. His farmhand, Zhao Da, was skilled in minor surgical tasks. Once, someone called him and said, "A certain family is hosting a gathering and needs you to perform." Zhao replied, "I am only skilled in singing funeral dirges. What use would that be for you?" The caller said, "The host wants to hear this song and will reward you generously." Zhao was surprised by the unusual request but felt he could not refuse. He secretly carried a bell and a gong in his sleeves and went. Upon arriving, he found the place very close to his home. He thought that there was no such household there before, and the house was narrow and small. Moreover, a funeral dirge was not appropriate to be heard at a drinking party, which made him even more suspicious. Just as he was about to play the bell and sing, over twenty men and women sitting down said in unison, "We all know the tune well, so there's no need for you to play it!" So he sang several verses without accompaniment, and they all clapped in rhythm and praised him, drinking merrily to the point of being half drunk. They asked again, "Can you perform any other arts?" Zhao said he could recite the Great Compassion Mound of Filth Dharma Spell. They all said they did not need it. Zhao suddenly realized they were dealing with spirits, so he reached for his bell, struck it, and loudly recited the mantra. Before he could finish a few lines, a cold, eerie wind swept through, and all the lamps, candles, and household items vanished, leaving everything pitch black. Looking up, he saw a small opening in the roof, just wide enough for a person to pass through. Moonlight filtered through, allowing him to see the surroundings, though he had to squeeze out sideways to get a clear view. The next day, he examined the location and found it overgrown with thorny bushes and tangled vegetation, revealing that it was an ancient tomb.

A traditional account states that there was a poem inscribed on the column of the Jiangting pavilion in Jingzhou, which reads: "With the curtain drawn back, I lean alone on the curved railing; the landscape stretches to the horizon at dusk, my tears have not dried. My home lies at the border of Wu and Chu, a few snowflakes fall haphazardly, startling the seagulls. The line of poetry is about to be completed, but it sinks into the haze." Huang Ruzhi read it with a sense of melancholy and said, "It seems to have been written for me. Who could have composed it? The style resembles that of a woman, and the line 'my tears have not dried' makes me suspect it was written by a spirit." That night, he dreamed of a woman who said, "I come from the mountains of Wucheng near Yuzhang. I traveled on a passenger boat and drowned here, unable to return home. I wrote this poem upon reaching the Jiangting pavilion, moved by my emotions. I did not expect you to recognize it." Huang Ruzhi woke up in shock and said, "This must be one of the small dragon girls from Wucheng!" This was during the first year of the Jianzhongjingguo era (1101 AD).

In the sixth year of the Gandaodao era (1167 CE), Wu Mingke served as the governor of Yuzhang (another name for Nanchang). His son passed the imperial examination. Zhu Jingwen, a fellow examinee from Qingjiang, came to meet by chance and was appointed to serve as the magistrate of Xinjian County. Within the prefectural administration, the Dragon King Temple in Wu City was being renovated, and Zhu was ordered to oversee the work. He was very strict and meticulous. When it came to creating new statues, Zhu pointed to the image of a goddess painted on the wall and told the craftsmen, "They must resemble this to be good!" After several changes, the statues were finally made as bright, beautiful, and alluring as the model, and Zhu was very pleased. Then he recalled a poem from Jingzhou, thinking its tone was bitter, depressed, and melancholic, and it was not the elegant and transcendent demeanor of the Dragon Palace. Composing a poem in the form of "Yu Lou Chun," he wrote on the wall: "Jade steps, crystal chambers, and an ice-jade canopy, Floor-to-ceiling crystal curtains that do not rise. My home is far from the world's troubles, With clouds drifting and gentle breezes. Sometimes, I leisurely sail my boat, With misty hair and waves of green. At night, I return fully laden with the bright moon, Breaking through the vast expanse of glass."

In the night, Zhu dreamed of a grand procession with banners, streamers, and ceremonial cloths, accompanied by an impressive entourage. A covered carriage was being carried, and inside it sat a beautiful woman. A messenger announced that a dragon maiden had come to pay a visit. Zhu descended from the carriage to meet her. After a banquet, they slept together, and the experience felt like it lasted a day and a night. Her speech was graceful, and her demeanor was dignified. As she was about to leave, she spoke to Zhu: "You must not remember the events of the past. You were previously the young son of the King of the Southern Sea, Guangli. Because of your travels, you became my husband. I truly served as your wife. Now, although you have been reborn into the Zhu family, you still carry the karmic connection. Yet the memory of Wu City is still vivid in your mind. Therefore, your fortune is often associated with the region of Yuzhang. When you hold office in the Southern Sea, your earthly good fortune will be nearly exhausted, and then you will reunite with your former spouse. Knowing that you wrote the *Yulou Chun* poem, which corrected previous misunderstandings, I am deeply moved. If you did not recall our past journey, you would not have known about my family so intimately." After speaking, she departed with sorrow.

Upon waking, he hurriedly wrote an essay to record the event, but did not understand the meaning of the South Sea, and merely said, "Perhaps it was because of speaking out that he was exiled to that place one day?" From then on, every night when he returned from outside, he often heard laughter and talking inside the room. Gradually, he became sick and emaciated, and his family suspected that he was possessed by a ghost, and persuaded him to stop and return home. The next year, he came to Wu again for some matter, but the official had already left. Later, the governor, Gong Shizhi, appointed him to oversee the wine office, but soon he left due to family difficulties. After the mourning period, he was transferred to serve as the magistrate of Fenyi in Yuanzhou, and returned home after a short while. The scholars from the county who had studied under him before heard that he had returned to his hometown and came to pay respects, and they talked about the local customs and geography. They happened to mention that there was a temple to the King of the South Sea in front of the magistrate's office, and Zhu suddenly felt lost and confused. The next day, he fell ill and never recovered. He had never actually reached his official post, and he served in two temporary positions in Yuzhang. So-called "many benefits" were just like this. It seems that when he first carved the image and wrote the inscription, his heart fell into a delusional state, thus severing his own life. The dream of the goddess, perhaps, was surely a cunning ghost pretending to deceive him. A person from Le Ping named Yang Zhen, who served as the magistrate of Linjiang, spoke about this matter in great detail.

In the thirty-first year of the Shaoxing era, a fisherman from Huzhou named Wu Yi tied his boat to a house outside the boundary of a new city's gate while fishing. At midnight, he heard people on the shore talking: "We have been staying in this house for a long time, we should plan to leave, how about moving into this boat?" Someone replied, "This is just a fishing boat, besides, it belongs to someone from another place, how can we board it? Tomorrow, a ship will come from the southeast, there will be two red lacquer boxes and several bottles of red clay wine on the ship. He is a relative of the sick person, coming to visit and inquire, and his family is quite wealthy, they will provide what we need." Everyone agreed, and then silence fell. Wu Yi found it strange. As dawn was breaking, he got up to investigate the matter. It turned out that the house was indeed suffering from an epidemic, and what they were discussing were ghosts. He then went directly to a spot several miles southeast and moored his boat to verify the information. Indeed, he met a small boat coming, the items on the boat matched what the ghosts had said. He quickly shouted to stop the boat and told them about what he had heard at night. They were greatly startled and said, "It is my son-in-law's family, I am just going to visit them to see if they are sick. If it were not for your help, the whole family would be recorded in the Book of the Dead!" He gave all the wine and food he was carrying as a gift, expressed thanks, and returned. (This story was told by Jing Pei's younger brother.)

Cheng Changyu, a general during the Southern Song dynasty, was dismissed from his post in Dingshou and returned to his hometown in Poyang. He stayed temporarily in Zijigong (Purple Extreme Palace), where his family resided in the main hall while he managed a room on the northern side by himself. During hot nights, he often slept in that room. One night, he saw two women wearing special hairstyles (特髻) enter the room from the bamboo forest and approach the bed. Cheng Changyu, who had experience leading troops and possessed great courage, recognized them as ghosts but felt no fear. He called out to them, asking, "Who are you, and why are you here at night?" They laughed but did not answer. Cheng Changyu then said, "If you have nothing else to do, could you massage my waist?" They agreed and moved forward, with Cheng Changyu extending his legs and allowing them to perform the massage. He soon fell into a deep sleep. When he woke up, it was already morning, and he saw nothing. However, the areas where they had massaged him were bruised and painful, and the pain took several days to subside. This account was told by Cheng Changyu's son, Xi.

Jì Yuánhéng (Nán Shòu), a native of Jǐnyún, immediately passed the imperial examination and was appointed as a teacher in Taizhou. He was planning to go to Jiankang to pay respects to the governor. At home, he had a concubine who was resentful because the main wife could not tolerate her, and she often harbored thoughts of suicide. When he was about to leave, Jì sincerely prayed to his wife, saying, "After I leave, please do not subject her to cruel treatment. If something unexpected happens, it might be too late to take care of her. If it becomes absolutely necessary, I will send her away when I return, which won't be difficult." His wife replied, "Just go with peace of mind; I won't do anything like that." At that time, they were temporarily living elsewhere (the location is not remembered). After several days, he arrived in Jiankang, having unloaded his baggage, and heard a whispering voice near his ear, which seemed to be that of his concubine, but he could not see her shape. When he asked, she wept and said, "As soon as you stepped out, I was beaten, and I could no longer live, so I hanged myself." Jì mourned and apologized, wanting to turn back, but thinking that he had already arrived, he wanted to disbelieve it but couldn't bear to, so he sent a servant back home to check the matter and also had the servant write a document for the family. Passing through the county, the servant still briefly mentioned the cost of burial. From then on, Jì wept every night. When the servant returned, he said that there was indeed nothing wrong at home, and when he arrived, the servant brought food for him. Jì said, "Then it must be a false spirit pretending to deceive me!" That evening, she came again, and Jì sternly rebuked her, saying, "What kind of evil spirit are you? How dare you not leave immediately? I will summon Taoist priests to punish you according to the law!" She replied, "It's not really her. Because when you first left, you had doubts, so I took the opportunity to create a false appearance. Now I just ask for a few scrolls of Buddhist scriptures and a small offering of paper money."

Xing Taifu (Xiaoyang), upon his initial southern migration, temporarily resided in a station in Deqing, Huzhou. The place was damp and narrow, insufficient for his family's needs, and he had a strong desire to settle in Lin'an. He acquired a mansion within the inner gate of Wang Taifu’s mansion for 3,000 strings of cash. Someone remarked, "In the capital city, such mansions are worth 50,000 strings of cash, yet now it costs only one-tenth of that. Do you also know why? This house has been possessed by evil spirits for a long time, and people can no longer live there, so it was given away!" Xing Taifu's wife and her mother-in-law, the Princess of Celebration, both agreed that it was unusable. Concerned, Xing Taifu gently told his wife, "Whether people's words are true or not, it is still uncertain. I will first test it. If I say there is nothing wrong, you will not believe me. Why not choose two trustworthy maids to go together, to prove its truth." So he went. They stayed for half a month, visiting every corner inside and outside the house, including hidden and remote places. At night, they slept in the main hall and experienced no fear at all. Upon returning, he told the whole family and strongly criticized the previous claim. The two maids also highly praised the beauty of the house. Finally, the entire family moved in.

On the day of the ghost festival, strange and supernatural phenomena appeared, and family members engaged in private conversations in the inner chambers of their house. These conversations were immediately responded to from the roof, with teasing, mocking, and indecent behavior occurring without interruption, even to the point of bewitching concubines, unconcerned and without avoiding people. As a result, everyone regretted their presence. At that time, Song Anguo was in Deqing, and he urgently summoned someone to perform rituals. They investigated and captured one ghost, chaining and imprisoning it in the northern darkness. Soon after, another ghost appeared, and they captured and punished it again. The ghost then reported that it had four brothers, as well as relatives from other clans and a large number of peers. Song Anguo had exhausted all his methods and became tired, so he told Xing Shi, "This place has undergone changes due to the violence of soldiers and captives, with countless people killed. How can we handle it today? It would be better to establish a grand ritual to rescue and liberate the spirits. I will lead the ceremony, making them transcendently reborn, so they do not become a source of resentment for the ghosts. This is also the best plan!" Xing Shi followed his advice and donated two million coins to hold the ritual. On the night of the banquet and purification, they placed a jar on a stand, covered it with cloth, and summoned all the stagnant spirits to gather inside. Song Anguo asked Xing Shi to personally carry the jar to the front, but despite their best efforts, they could not lift it. They only felt a rustling sound inside, like the movement of crabs. After the ceremony, eight strong soldiers carried the jar out, all saying that it was very heavy on their shoulders, as if they were carrying a hundred pounds each. They buried it in a deep pit in the bamboo garden, and the house has remained peaceful and quiet ever since (this matter was told by Zhao Yanzé).

Li Bing's son Yong, throughout his life, habitually printed banners inscribed with the Supreme Victory Dharani and burned them as offerings to the realm of spirits. In the sixth year of the Chunxi era (1176 AD), he served as an inspector at the office of mining and smelting, stationed in Xinzhou. The following December, he was summoned to Yiyang, where the magistrate invited him to drink. After returning and already having slept, he lay down in the official inn, barely asleep, when he saw two women, one wearing a hat and the other with a bun, wandering back and forth in the hall. Recognizing them as unusual beings, he did not question them further. A little while later, he barely woke up to find them near his bed. He prayed to them, saying, "If you have something to say, please tell me clearly. Otherwise, do not disturb my sleep." After turning over, he fell asleep again and dreamed that the two women came to bow and said, "We have no request of you, only we wish to receive the habitually printed Dharani banners." Yong replied, "I am on duty and did not bring them with me. What can be done about it?" They said, "Wait for you to return to Xinzhou and give them to you is not too late." He asked for their names, and they said, "There is no need to ask; you will know them yourself." The next morning, he visited various people, and one of his traveling companions, a military officer, said, "In recent years, a concubine of the governor Lu Yi of Guangzhou died due to childbirth in this room. The one with the bun is her, but the one with the hat is unknown." Upon returning to his lodging, Yong printed several dozen copies and burned them for them.

The official residence of a professor in Wenzhou had a small building that was occupied by ghostly things. These ghosts frequently threw broken tiles and stones, sighed, sang, and shouted, and often wrote on small pieces of paper and threw them outside, making it too frightening for anyone to enter. In the twenty-ninth year of the Shaoxing era, the professor from Wuyuan, Li Zhiyin Zhizhong, intended to investigate the strange occurrences. He set up a desk, paper, and writing tools in the small building, joined two sheets of paper together, and wrote on them: "What are you? Perhaps you are the lingering spirit of a deceased person, not yet attained liberation. Since you can write, you should provide your entire life story to me. I will have monks recite scriptures to help elevate you. Do not linger here and cause harm to the owner." That evening, he personally locked the door.
